text_cleaned: a return to form for capcom: this is the best game capcom has made in almost 10 years, possibly more when considering how much they used to strive in creating unique game mechanics, innovating within a genre that is in need of a breath of fresh air. pragmata is exactly that and then some. pragmata s unique gameplay style is unabashedly capcom with fun and thrilling moment to moment gameplay i ve not seen in a shooter since lost planet 2006 , added on by a story that is emotionally investing, genuine and to the heart. it s not a great story as with most capcom titles, but the storytelling through hugh and diana s bond makes it possibly the best narrative they ve done in years, arguably better than joel and ellie. watching people react and start longing to create family of their own, is proof that video games are impactful and pragmata s message is one that should be protected. we don t get games like this often. to see this game succeed is a site to be hold and one that should be cherished. it s not common for a brand new ip to achieve what pragmata does in an industry that has long since lost it s soul and drive to make games that have an impact, not just in story but in gameplay. as a long time capcom fan since 2006, longing for a new sci fi ip since lost planet is no longer with us, this is the best game they ve produced and everyone working on it should be proud. you did it. please make more games like this. thank you, capcom and the pragmata team.

text_cleaned: i ll be updating this review as i progress, but here are my early impressions. i m just past the tutorial, and the game is really starting to open up. graphically, it s beautiful and extremely well optimized. a welcome surprise these days. the combat took a little adjusting. you re essentially juggling two forms of combat and need to switch between them throughout each encounter. i d strongly recommend playing with a controller over mouse and keyboard for this one. the overall feel reminds me of dead space, though it s not nearly as scary or grotesque. the story is there, but the writing can feel cheesy at times, and the main character s voice performance comes across as a bit flat. that said, this is likely a product of the japanese to english translation and is about par for the course with capcom titles. as for tension, while i wouldn t call them jumpscares exactly, encounters can and will catch you off guard even when you re expecting them. if you ve been feeling like modern gaming has gone stale with overhyped multiplayer titles that all blur together, poorly optimized launches, and rushed, broken releases, this game is a breath of fresh air. it feels like a throwback to the xbox 360 and ps3 era, when studios focused on storytelling and shipped a finished product. i highly recommend giving it a shot.

text_cleaned: pragmata is one of the most refreshing games i ve played in a while. right from the start, it stands out with how well optimized it is on pc. performance is smooth and consistent, which already puts it ahead of many modern releases. the characters are another strong point. they have distinct personalities and are genuinely easy to like, which makes the story more engaging. on the gameplay side, things feel fresh and innovative. the controls are extremely fluid, and everything just clicks in a satisfying way. the hacking mechanics add a unique layer of challenge, it takes a bit of getting used to at first, but once it clicks, it feels very natural within the gameplay loop of hacking enemies and then taking them down. stylistically, the game gives off vibes similar to dead space and stellar blade, but it still manages to carve out its own identity. at its core, it keeps that classic capcom style resource management very reminiscent of resident evil which adds tension without feeling overwhelming. one thing i really appreciated is the difficulty balance. even on the hardest lunatic difficulty, the game is challenging but never feels punishing read end. i changed my mind . it doesn t come across like it s trying to frustrate the player, which is a welcome change compared to a lot of newer titles. my only real complaint is that i wish there was more of it. the game could definitely benefit from a longer runtime and more environments to explore. final score: 9 10

text_cleaned: this game was an almost perfect game for me. i really enjoyed the story, but the main part was the father daughter dynamic between the characters. the gameplay was also excellent, and unique. it takes a bit to get into a flow state with the mini game while also trying to attack enemies. the challenge was at the perfect level for me on standard. i do not enjoy games that force you to die over and over again, and i am not a not a believer in the get good mentality that some games seem to have. i believe in difficult settings so that people can choose how to play the game. that being said, the one complaint i have is that the hard difficulty lunatic is locked until you complete the game. while i never play on hard, i always go for the standard, i believe that those who want a challenge from the beginning should be allowed that option. time wise, this took me about 20 hours to complete, i did have not completed the unknown signal missions yet, and i haven t decided if i want to. while some might complain that isn t long enough for a 70 dollar game, i would much rather have a great 20 hours of gameplay vs 300 hours of just bland side missions. graphics and performance wise, this game was mostly great. the visuals were great, and the performance for the most part was also very smooth, even for my older 3080. there were a few fps drops in the mines, but i am sure those will be smoothed out in a few updates since the rest of the game performed very well.

text_cleaned: the positive sentiment surrounding this game confuses me. from what i ve seen, it usually boils down to two things: diana is so cute! and wow, this hacking thing is cool and different! i went into this game with those things in mind and found myself feeling the exact opposite. for me, diana is absolutely ruined by the voice acting. i can t stand the adult doing a child voice. it s like nails on a chalkboard. the japanese version of her is a little better, since the japanese language has structures built into it to help sell the this character is a child feeling. but. you then get japanese hugh, which i like less than english hugh. ideally i d want to mix languages, but that isn t possible. thus, most of the events cutscenes that were supposed to be endearing just felt annoying to me. my advice is to really listen to the voices in the trailers and decide if you are an irrationally sensitive individual like myself before purchasing. next point, the hacking. cool, different, tedious. the dual control scheme felt fresh for a while, but it very quickly became annoying to hack the same enemies over and over. the game acknowledges this and does give you some tools to mitigate it, like multi hack nodes and auto hacking, but the repetition is still fundamentally there. due to this hacking focus, all enemies that i ve encountered thus far are very slow and highly telegraphed. trivial, aside from managing the hacking minigame. this moves the focus of combat from killing the enemies as quickly as possible to killing them as efficiently as possible. this is the true innovation, i think, since each enemy type becomes a puzzle to pick apart. summarily, the innovation is in combat design, not hacking. hacking is mostly a superfluous add on to facilitate interaction with diana. the combination of repetitive hacking and an annoying sidekick make this game a hard sell for me. the combat design is interesting, but i feel like it is held down a bit by the ever present hacking minigame. tldr; if you don t mind diana s voice acting, like children for some crazy reason , and don t mind some repetition, go ahead, buy the game. you ll like it.

text_cleaned: this is the ultimate weekend with dad simulator a 10 hour propaganda journey to make western people want to have kids again. but in all honesty, it s an amazing game. you play as an astronaut maintenance guy sent to fix things on a moon base. obviously, everything goes wrong, and you and a little android kid have to save the world. the gameplay is great. i played on a controller, and even though i have terrible aim with one, i managed just fine. i also liked the idea of always needing two separate combat components to take down enemies hacking and shooting. movement feels crisp, too. the graphics are mostly beautiful. some close ups are weirdly low quality, but the moon base and the lunar surface look stunning. performance is also solid almost everything on max, 1440p, 100 texture scaling, around 130 fps on average . the story starts off really simple but ramps up a bit. the biggest motivation is definitely keeping diana entertained. the game is only about 10 hours long, which might be a downside for some. still, i d say it s worth every penny, if you ve got the budget for it. all in all, a very good game in almost every department, except maybe length. 4.8 found rems for diana out of 5

text_cleaned: instead of spitting out an ai generated trash review that goes into more detail than the game deserves, i ll just give you my honest take for the honest buyer. the game is mid. it s surface level and little more than that. it s fine, and fun at times, but for a 60 dollar price of entry, and first game for this new ip , i ve gotta say, it s remarkably unremarkable. the story itself is nothing, and exists for the gameplay alone. the gameplay is somewhat fresh, but runs out of ideas quick, settling with giving you a plethora of upgrades and new abilities you don t even need because the game is simple braindead poo poo easy. 90 percent of the time you are walking through white gray sterilized scifi space station hallways. and just when you think they re mixing up the environments, they fall back into white gray hallways. people say the game looks great, but the overall art direction is so mid that the game falls into looking quite ugly i think. the star of the show, and the selling point of this game, is the ai child diana, who they really don t do much with, nor do they make any attempts of actually developing her character or making the player want to know more. you can run through all possible free time chats with her in the safe room within 3 minutes, and it runs down into her repeating little events and cutscenes over and over again. the game is great at casting the illusion of their being a lot of content, but that s all it is, an illusion. behind the veil there is very little going on, and the game feels unfinished and bare bones. the overly positive reviews genuinely bewilder me as they are extremely glazy, to the point that i feel they are hired bot reviews or mossad agents. regardless, they all read like ai prompts and fail to come across as any kind of genuine. i mean, at least it s not 70 dollars. unless you bought the deluxe edition, you didn t do that did you?

text_cleaned: gamers get the journalists they deserve. 90 of this game so far is forced pacing aka braindead filler encounters. the game is built around a gimmick that a competent person with a working brain would discard immediately before building an entire game around it what if you had to hold a button and move the mouse over a grid in order to be able to do any meaningful damage? oh, that s obviously stupid? okay, forget it. it s a mechanic with no potential whatsoever that is tired within about ten minutes of gameplay. generally you can negate the danger of encounters by using your timed i frame dodge for melee attacks and running around enough to thwart the ranged enemies tracking, so any potential depth is thwarted. after a few hours i m struggling to continue playing. it is nonstop boredom. the boss fights are also very shallow bullet sponges. even the story and the messages emails you can find are such generic regurgitated sci fi cliche slop that i can t be bothered to read them. i can promise you that there is something better for you to play than this.

text_cleaned: ebert s ghost continues to haunt the discussions of whether the videogames are actually art they are , but once in a while you will play something that makes you consider that there was at least a kernel of truth. this is hard to evaluate as anything beyond a digital toy. comparing it with the tone of the reveal trailer, i wonder if it got focus grouped to death, or maybe spending 6 years in development forced them into making something as extremely inoffensive as possible so it would alienate the least amount of people and bring in the money. the interactions of the main duo are cute and decently well written, but it s just dad gaming and you probably have either played or seen or read it done better, which is a really easy bar to clear. the story proper is written in incredibly broad strokes and makes it hard to connect to anything happening, or even the plight of the main antagonist, whom you see in person for the combined total of 3 times. one thing of note though, albeit not enough to move the needle for me personally, is that it got a much more jovial hopeful tone than general dad gaming; your tlous, gow and sanabis are all about a depressed dad finally finding some light in life, but this one is distinct by having the main character just be a regular guy. he s had a happy upbringing, he loves space, and so he simply wants to work the with company to realize his dream of going there. things do go awry, he is perturbed by what s happening, but he isn t here to resolve his long seated personal grudges or try to escape reality, which is a decent change of pace. the actual gameplay is pretty fun though, it throws new stuff at you often enough to not get stale, the enemy variety is solid and the actual arenas can be pretty good too. the hacking is an actually fun mechanic, and it only starts getting on you by the time the game is already ends. it did not exactly compel me to play through all the training stages and the additional episode though. decently fun gameplay coupled with a deeply perfunctory story as a whole package is as :60: as it gets. i didn t dislike my time with it, but it gets no recommendation from me.

it makes me pretty much enjoy the game, and you have to time well between whether you would like to shoot or hack first or do both!. i play on both controller and mouse keyboard. for me, i felt the controller gave smoother control and a better experience, but if you want faster hacking, i think mouse keyboard would be a better choice. plus, if you re using a ps controller, it has an adaptive trigger as well. i finished the story around 12 hours with all the side quest training program which you probably found challenging , and mostly completed 80 the achievement. you would also unlock a new mode after you finished the story i haven t tried it yet . b story b to be honest, i think the story at the start of the game was pretty bland and predictable, just keep shooting robots and walking to another sector. this is where i minus the point, but it gives you a pretty much wholesome feeling with a father daughter moment, which is pretty much cute af to me! after half a game, the story just exponentially revealed a lot of things, and the story turned out to be more encouraging for me to make a longer run to find out what would happen next, compared to the first half. and after i finished the game, i would say this one has a better story than re9 itself! my personal thought tho b graphics b the graphics were pretty much great. if you have ever played a capcom game before, it s not much different, pretty much the same as re9. i could run smoothly on my pc specification included in this review around 80 fps on high ultra setting without frame gen, with only a few laggy frames while loading a new scene. b in conclusion b i love this game sooooo much. i bought it because of the gameplay i ve tried on the demo, but didn t expect such a superb experience i got after finishing the game! text_cleaned: pragmata is a beautiful game with a great cast of characters, a unique combat mechanic, and multiple tragedies rolled into one story. i was initially drawn to pragmata after seeing its hacking based combat mechanic. while it takes some time to get used to before you re comfortably moving around the battlefield and taking down rogue ai left and right, once it clicks, it becomes one of the most enjoyable shooters i ve played in years. i ve seen another review mention that the combat is reminiscent of alan wake, and i have to agree. the slower, survival horror style combat fits perfectly within the control scheme i played with a ps5 controller and had no issues at all. there are really only three main characters, with a fourth appearing through hologram recordings that help tell the story leading up to the game s events. the experience is largely carried by the relationship between hugh and dianna. capcom absolutely nailed the father daughter dynamic, and i found myself caring about them, and smiling or laughing at their interactions, much sooner than i typically do. the gameplay and characters are a solid 10 10, but the story and level design are where it loses a few points for me. without spoilers, many levels boil down to find a locked door and search the area for the unlock nodes. fortunately, the visual design of each level and the sheer fun of the combat are more than enough to keep things engaging. as for the story, the ending was fairly predictable, but it still managed to land emotionally. the comparatively weaker story and somewhat repetitive progression loop are the game s main drawbacks. i finished the main story in about two days, playing roughly six hours each day, and i still plan to go back for collectibles and aim for 100 completion, which isn t something i do very often anymore. overall, i d give the game an 8 10.

text_cleaned: update: remove winedetectionenabled:false from the launch args for the game to boot. no rt though. update: unfixable boot crash due to denuvo on any all linux distros. ggs. recommendation status: updated to no from yes . let me start off by saying this: i am not a big fan of story games. i have autism adhd, so my usual go to s are automation or sandbox games i have about 500 hours in satisfactory, for context . even when i do enjoy a story driven game like portal, the second it stops being strictly linear like trying to escape the test chambers , i get completely lost and frustrated. but pragmata? this game made me feel things i never have before. it was emotional, thrilling, sad, and touching all at once. the progression is somewhat linear, which kept me perfectly on track without wandering aimlessly, and the combat system requires constant multitasking which kept my adhd brain completely locked in. on top of that, the incredible sound design and music just elevate all of it, adding so much depth to the feeling and atmosphere of the game. to put how much the story impacted me into perspective: i really do not like children. seriously, i despise them. so the fact that this game actually made me want to protect diana, and made me feel like i needed to keep her safe, is a huge testament to how well she is written. from her adorable character design to my pure hatred towards idus and that one laser shooting robot, fug you , this game has managed to tickle every part of my brain. on a technical level, the visuals are hyper realistic and surprisingly performant natively. a quick heads up for my fellow linux users: dlss, frame gen, and upscaling crashed the game for me, and turning on path tracing literally caused wine to display death messages lmao . but honestly, you don t even need them. running natively, i managed a rock steady 165fps vsync cap on my rtx 4070 ti super, and a solid 80 100fps on my 3070 ti machine though it dropped a bit in big open spaces . i actually cried for like 30 minutes during and after the ending. it was... it was something alright. i cannot stress enough how good this game is. p.s. yea, i use em dashes so what?

text_cleaned: pragmata: no spoiler review tldr: pragmata is very standard video game fare. it says something truly sad about aaa video game culture that a very average video game release is met with near universal praise simply because it isn t broken at launch and has a standard entertaining gameplay loop with good characters. the level design is an annoying mess of unremarkable corridors and shafts, the platforming is slow and droll, and the gameplay boils down to hack, dodge, and shoot. all these aspects are perfectly serviceable but not incredible. diana certainly steals the show. the more you like her, the more you ll like the game. she s certainly cute and actually acts like a kid in all the best ways. she s designed to be the absolute pinnacle of what a lovable child could be. this means that anything kids do that s grating or annoying simply isn t present. the story is as follows. investigate space station something went wrong shoot things escape. it s likely even more cliche than a knight saving a maiden from a dragon at this point. the game only took 12 hours to beat, and i wasn t trying to go very fast. there doesn t appear to be any replay ability besides getting all the collectibles and higher difficulties. as for why i wouldn t recommend it? 60 is too much for a standard 7 10 video game when everyone has a backlog. when thousands of 8 10, 9 10, and 10 10 video games exist, it s hard to justify spending 60 on something that is the lowest tier of what most gamers would consider to be a good video game. difficulty on its surface pragmata is an easy game. the difficulty often comes from overwhelming the player with numbers. the hardest part of the game is the beginning when you can t often heal yourself and you re unfamiliar with the gameplay loop. mid game is absurdly easy. later, the difficulty goes up again for the endgame. now for a pet peeve. there is no reason to force the player to return to the homebase every time they die. i really don t feel the need to watch diana play with a new toy every time i m forced back to shelter. instead of restarting me at my last checkpoint, capcom decides you need to essentially wait 60 80 seconds to get back into the action again. it s intentionally done to try to make you spend more time in the shelter. but i hate it. gameplay as stated above in my tldr, this game boils down to playing a hacking mini game, shooting, and dodging. of course there are nuances like separating enemies so you can fight them 1v1. but realistically the combat depth isn t really there. by the end of the game it was getting pretty old. especially since enemies are incredibly tanky. i timed a streamer s winning attempts while fighting bosses; they were generally 10 15 minutes long. during that time you need to dodge many hundreds of attacks. having to retry when you fail 10 minutes in is brutal. platforming honestly functions well. but it s not really my cup of tea. hugh is slow and bulky. i much prefer a faster and more precise type of platforming. like in recore 2016 . story and writing while the overarching story is as cliche as it gets, the worldbuilding is neat. humanity finds a new element on the moon that can replicate nearly any material. this stuff is super common, and they use it to build a gargantuan 3d printer on the moon as a research facility. i d be lying if i said it wasn t a cool idea. the real good stuff comes from the writing and character interactions. there are a ton of conversations between diana and hugh that are all fully voice acted. it ranges from lighthearted to educational, philosophical, serious, etc. while hugh does treat diana like a kid. he never treats her like a moron or as someone unserious. he s respectful and honorable; she is kind and sympathetic. these two really embody the best of humanity, and it s pretty cool to see. i can t spoil anything, but i m a big fan of the game s ending. graphics and audio i ain t the expert on graphics. it looks good, damn good even. i kept that ray tracing and frame gen crap off and ran the game at 4k 60fps. it looked pretty when i wasn t in bland looking areas with nothing but white walls. these sci fi games really love their boring aesthetics, huh? the music is fine; i don t remember any of it. the sound design is once again just fine. if you re expecting id software type sound design, then you ll be disappointed. level design this is the weakest part of pragmata. the level design screams videogame at the top of its lungs everywhere you go. corridors with no purpose, random shafts everywhere, paths that lead to nothing, etc. the majority of this space station seems to have been built to make sure its employees get enough cardio. but the worst sin are the objectives. nearly every area in this game requires you to activate 2 8 terminals in an area to progress. it isn t an issue to do this the first or second time. but a dozen times in, it quickly becomes apparent that this game is a bit of a one trick pony when it comes to level design philosophy. miscellaneous i didn t realize that opening ladders were the equivalent of checkpoints. so i ran past them all and wondered why level 3 expected me to complete a level with dozens of combat encounters while having so little health. it s probably just user error. but regardless, it was very frustrating, i won t lie. the aiming and camera sensitivity does not go half as high as it should. i guess they wanted hugh to be a slug because if you want that camera turned fast, you ll have to click the left stick to reorient the camera behind him. we re going back to 1998 gaming mechanics with this one boys. enemies can stun lock and combo you to death. this pisses me off. sometimes diana talks too much. how common for a modern video game. in a certain bossfight she spammed the same voice line at least 30 50 times. the finisher animations are all totally lame. maybe doom 2016 spoiled me with those awesome glory kills. when hugh punches a big metal robot with what looks like a small taser and it inexplicably shatters into a thousand pieces, it s just weird. this is 99 of the games finisher moves. summary pragmata is a perfectly acceptable video game. if you think it s worth 60 plus tax, that s fine by me. i found there just wasn t any big wow factor anywhere. i plan to 100 this game. if i have any further thoughts i want to add to this review, i ll post them in the comments.

text_cleaned: this game is the typical aaa minimum viable product. it s not bad, but it s not really good either. still, i think a lot of mainstream players could enjoy it. it s just not for me, and there are reasons why. honestly, i don t think this game deserves its price for what it offers. the ideas used here could have been so much better, and i expected more. diana is really adorable, and i thought the game would explore the subject of ai, and how a human and an ai could grow closer and understand each other or not . yet this concept is barely used at all, and instead it just serves as an excuse to give you a companion that hacks things. the interactions are minimal and don t really make the player bond with the girl. it s all very surface level. like don t expect last of us 1. the sci fi aspect is also almost nonexistent. it s a basic concept used to explain the environment, but it does nothing to build an immersive world. this game is also really repetitive, and in a bad way. the gameplay loop of door locked fight x enemies to unlock it repeat until the door opens fight the boss go to the next stage gets boring very quickly. the backtracking for chests is also not fun, and the rewards are not worth the time honestly. it s better to just rush through the game without exploring anything. basic features like teleportation to known levels or healing at checkpoints are not even there. you need to go back to the refuge every time for that, which adds loading screens constantly. i guess it s meant to force interactions with diana, but it just feels bad because there aren t many interactions, and the ones that exist are really shallow anyway. i m really not enjoying the game anymore. after the first hour, when it was still enjoyable, it quickly became boring and fell flat. a solid 6 10, decent enough to function as a viable product, with the concept of printing things and having an ai child mainly there to feel different and help sell the game. i can t recommend it at this price when the gameplay is shallow, the exploration is nonexistent and annoying, and the story is uninteresting. maybe i m just spoiled from playing too many indie games, but i find it frustrating to have an experience like this presented to me at 60 . out of the 19 hours i spent in this game, i was afk for at least half of it, doing something else because i kept needing breaks out of boredom. i honestly felt scammed, like i paid a premium price for a tiny hacking mini game stretched into a full release. at this point, i d rather be playing dice in kingdom come: deliverance.

text_cleaned: capcom used to stick with safe releases. new monster hunter or street fighter game, remaking a resident evil title that was already good and doesn t need remaking, maybe even making a new resident evil game to varying levels of quality. but in the past few years, they ve started to spice things up with unexpected things. less than two years ago they released kunitsu gami: path of the goddess a fantastic and weird mix of hack and slash action and tower defense. this year, the surprise is pragmata a very curious mix of different ideas that starts really well and ends alright. since the most interesting thing about pragmata is its gameplay structure and mechanics, i will start with them. for all intents and purposes, this is an over the shoulder third person shooter, but you have a lot of movement options like jumping and dodging and, most importantly, you can and must hack enemies during the combat. which isn t done in the style of nier: automata, where the gameplay switches to an entirely different mini game during hacking, but rather as you aim at the enemies, the hacking ui is added to the gameplay and you have to move through the nodes of that ui while the rest of the gameplay is happening. it seems overwhelming at first, but for most of the game, this idea is handled really well, with easy to memorize general patterns and lots of tools that help you with the process. so by the end, you will be easily shooting, hacking, moving camera and dodging at the same time and it feels very fresh and fun. since i ve mentioned jumping, you can imagine that there are platforming challenges too. most of them are entirely optional and are about exploring the levels and finding cool stuff. since you will be looking for different upgrade elements and currency and also mods that bring passive or sometimes even active changes to your gameplay. there s an entire separate collection of challenging training missions too, which are clearly inspired by the metal gear death stranding vr missions. which brings us to structure, because if hacking is easy to understand even from trailers and screenshots, the structure of the game turned out to be different from what i ve expected. in a way, the game reminded me of demon s souls mixed with ideas from different mega man titles. there s a shelter , which is a homebase you always start in and always return to if you die. and from that shelter, you can go to different locations that get unlocked as you progress through the game. in said locations you will be unlocking shortcuts, that serve as checkpoints, so you don t need to do the whole level from the start if you die. if you do die, all of the key fights do not respawn, all of the main progress through the level is saved but otherwise all normal enemies do respawn in the exact same locations and same ways as you ve encountered them the first time. as such, you re basically meant to go on these little expeditions into the level and progress until you feel content or die , after which you will return to the shelter to upgrade stuff, do optional things, grab a loadout of weapons and mods and go back into the level. oh and yeah, the weapons, apart from your main one, have limited ammo and are discarded once depleted, so you have to pick up stuff on the level and adapt all of the time. and the limited heals heal pretty poorly too, so everything about the game is designed to make you want to go back and regroup, as that will refill your health and resources for free. i have to say, for the first maybe two thirds of the game, this worked really well. the game isn t easy, but it s manageable on the normal difficulty, so i was getting a similar adventurous excitement to what i felt exploring demon s souls back in 2010. and the overall balance between the complexity of enemies and fights, given that you need to hack things and know how to utilize your weapons and mods effectively, was very solid. but the last third of the game becomes more messy and annoying than anything else. the balance is lost and some fights or enemy types and configurations become really annoying to deal with. even the training missions become quite unhinged at times, while for the most part they are very fun. so when i finally got to the ending and then unlocked the cool new post game mode and new game plus and a harder difficulty i just didn t want to continue. oh and speaking of the ending, or the story in general, it s as subtle as a trout slap to the face. game starts and within minutes our main character talks that robots are untrustworthy and kids are annoying, but then few minutes later becomes best friends and a father figure to a kid cyborg. every trope you can imagine follows suit. and look, capcom were never on the forefront of smart writing and at least this story isn t as insultingly moronic as what happens in resident evil requiem, but the potential for a genuinely good story was here, and it never comes. made me remember the fantastic binary domain, which had its own share of issues, but was brilliantly written in comparison. but hey, it is presented and told well enough. the music is solid. the visuals are wonderful. so the story being primitive isn t that distracting. pragmata is absolutely worth playing, especially if you ve checked the demo and enjoyed it. it would benefit from a slight rebalance and polishing for the later stages and the training missions, but even if capcom decide not to do so, the game is good as is. i ended up enjoying it less than how much i expected to, but i still enjoyed it a lot. oh and, there are so many little things that are reminiscent of dino crisis 3 it s genuinely odd. it was a dreadfully boring game, but there were some neat ideas, so it is nice to see them not fully forgotten. anyway pragmata is a great new experiment from capcom and while it doesn t land as gracefully as kunitsu gami, it s still worth playing.

text_cleaned: warning, this review will have spoiler and is more about how this game affected me: this is funny that all the way back in 2021, i played resident evil village to take my mind off during the covid and the time my family had a lot of things going on. to put it short, i expected horror and action to numb me during that time and played the game as a life long resident evil fan, but i did not expect the story to give me a more positive outlook in life, namely what i could still do during such an unstable time. ethan s story inspired me to stay determined even in the unknown and trying time. fast forward to 2026, i played pragmata due to loving capcom s games and expected the game to be an adventure about a human father and his android daughter on moon. i was playing the game to distract me away the anxiety from many things, from finding jobs and relationships with other people. yet, the story and its conclusion gave me hope to keep on pushing in this trying time. hugh and diana inspired to leave the world better than we found it even if you are hurt. thank you so much for everything. i am not just a happier person, but i have the courage to keep on going. your games changed me.

text_cleaned: capcom has done it yet again... delayed 3 times, pragmata has somehow managed to come out and completely surprised me. from its gameplay down to the characters, i loved every bit and flaw of this game. with the gameplay, i was easily won over with its unique hacking system and variety of enemies. at first i was worried the hacking would be too much for my brain to take on, but i was so wrong. it s really fun to pull off, especially once you get cool hacking nodes items that act as hacks . much of the enemies gave me horizon zero dawn vibes, while the weapons and tools took me back to dead space a little. there s variety to tailor to your kind of playstyle. before playing i ve heard some mixed things about the story and characters, but i personally ended up loving both, especially the characters. hugh and diana, the main two, are the icing on the cake. as the game progressed i became attached to them. i loved the quiet moments between the two characters, the optional but super wholesome interactions at the game s safe room the shelter . hugh and diana helped elevate it all for me. this is the beauty of video games to me. we all have different opinions. we all connect to different games, characters, and stories. pragmata just happened to be one that spoke to me. capcom, how do you do it?

text_cleaned: everybody plays games for their own reasons. some like to delve deep into the gameplay and learn all about the moves, combos upgrades and weapons. others just like to find all collectibles and enjoy their time with the story. i m the latter. i finished the game and did all areas 100 . then i realized there was another ending to the game. if you don t care about the story at all and it s just a game bro to you then this review might not be for you and it s best to read other ones that focus on gameplay. i focus on story and i find story resolution very important after investing 12 hours into a game s plot and characters. and for that reason i can t recommend this game when the true ending is locked behind endless grinding annoying challenge chambers. i m a completionist but i m also a 50 year old gamer. i don t have time, nor the reflexes or patience to go through all that just for a tiny little extra cutscene in the end which fixes the entire story. and i just hate that the true ending means endless hours of extremely annoying grind. fix the ending and i ll recommend it. until then if you care about reaching a proper conclusion to the story and hate grinding through endless challenge chambers to do it, don t buy this because it will disappoint you a lot in that area.

text_cleaned: pragmata leans heavily into that futuristic, almost sterile sci fi aesthetic clean environments, reflective surfaces, and a ton of detail in lighting. character models look sharp, especially in close ups, and the world design has that next gen only feel. lighting is really what stands out though. there s a lot of dynamic lighting, reflections, and effects that give everything a premium look. it s the kind of game where you can tell capcom is pushing modern hardware. at native 4k, this game looks ridiculous super crisp, no blur, just clean image quality across the board. gameplay feels different in a good way. there s a mix of action and what feels like light puzzle or tactical mechanics, especially depending on how they incorporate the companion system. it s not just run and gun you actually have to think a bit. movement feels solid, not overly fast but responsive. combat from what i ve experienced so far leans more strategic than chaotic. it s giving me a blend of: sci fi narrative focus controlled combat pacing and a bit of experimentation with mechanics definitely not your typical shooter.

text_cleaned: tl;dr: pragmata is a cute yet thrilling action game with a fun and interesting story. however, aside from crashing and fatal errors, combat leaves a bit to be desired, and the story doesn t explain a lot. ah, the masculine urge to be a father. in pragmata, you take on the role of hugh williams. hugh is part of a service tech team sent by earth based corporation delphi to their lunar base, cradle. the cradle has gone radio silent, and you re there to diagnose the issue and restore communications. as the store page will tell you, however, things don t quite go as planned. you re separated from your team, and it s only with the help of a strange android designed to look like a human child that you re able to get to safety. from there, your journey to get home begins. for those who don t care, a tl;dr is at the top of this review. for those who want my personal rundown, read on. h1 the story h1 now, i ll make this as spoiler free as possible, and any spoilers i do include will be minor, vague, and blotted out via spoiler text, if need be. the story itself is quite decent, and acts as a form of father daughter bonding experience between hugh and diana. surprisingly, it doesn t really follow a typical android becomes human story; for all her quirks, diana i does i act much like a human child, oddly enough. she does have a few moments where she doesn t understand human behavior or emotion, but they re few and far between. the story itself is linear, without much deviation or exposition: you re hugh williams, you were sent to check something out at delphi s moon base, went wrong, diana s now here well, she didn t just randomly appear , and you two are trying to get to earth now. you can find lore documents and recorded holographic conversations that provide some exposition and explanation for the cradle and its history, but there isn t a lot. for example, what spoiler dead filament spoiler is and how it came to be, with its properties shown in game, is never really explained. it s shown to you and plays a decently sized role in the story, but what it really is? no clue. also, why do they claim in the intro that lunafilament is a weak material, and yet, delphi is using it to print bots and spoiler military grade robots spoiler that are capable of tanking high powered weaponry and explosives? there are little bits and pieces that aren t shown or explained to the player, perhaps for future use in dlc or a sequel, but it s just as likely a plot hole. now, for how hugh and diana fit into the story: honestly, it s moreso diana s story than hugh s. diana has never been to earth, and has a connection to the cradle that hugh does not have. hugh has some story, but it takes a back seat to the story of trying to get back to earth and diana s personal growth. hugh is more of a plot device, despite being one of the main characters. but honestly? it s not like it s done i poorly i . hugh as a plot device could have been done a lot worse. like, in my opinion, i would ve preferred he had a true arc like diana, but for what they made? it ain t bad at all. h1 the cast h1 hugh and diana make for a fun duo. hugh is a gruff 40 ish year old man with a heart of gold; while he initially starts off a little cold to diana though he s well within his rights to be given what happened before their meeting, and he never takes it out on her , he swiftly warms up to her and treats her much like an overly hyperactive daughter. he ll answer her questions, treat her warmly, play with her, even tell her about his past and talk about philosophical issues with her much as they can with her understanding, anyways . though, i have to say, he i really i doesn t get much in the way of character development. if you re going through areas you ve already been, encounter points of interest, or interact with diana in the shelter while b she s b doing something, then hugh might get a little exposition as diana asks about his past or he comments on things he s done before. but it s almost all about his past, and rarely does he say anything about what he ll do when he gets back to earth. and he doesn t get much more than this elsewhere it s all mostly about diana. basically, hugh doesn t get much character development because, well, he s i already i a developed character. we just never got to see the development, save for the bare minimum. diana, for her part, acts like, well, an extremely curious child. she finds just about everything fascinating or fun, even when in danger. she s insatiably curious about earth, hugh, subject was, i can t recall . he can get snippy with her, but never to the point of anger, and only when she makes a truly spectacular faux pas. h1 the gameplay h1 the gameplay is... decent? okay, it i works i , it s balanced around how it s meant to play out, and it s decently fun when you get the hang of it. but honestly? i ve got mixed feelings, because it is not your traditional shooter game, and while that s fine, it took some getting used to. worse, i d say it s clunky. it isn t as smooth as i d have hoped combat is done via gunplay. high always carries a primary weapon that, rather than reloading or using ammo, recharges over the course of a few seconds. he also carries disposable secondary weapons that, once you re out of ammo, you discard them. these secondary weapons are either for attacking or tactical utility, and fill specific niches in their category. finally, hugh also carries a defensive tool to protect himself in combat. the catch? enemies are i extremely i resistant to damage. even a fully charged shot from the strongest gun in the game, when fully upgraded, won t kill the weakest enemy in the game. to deal any damage, you need to use the game s other combat function: hacking. when aiming at an enemy, diana will be able to hack them. by solving a grid puzzle, you forcibly open an enemy s outer shell, allowing you to damage the vulnerable insides. but doing the grid puzzle locks you from looking around, because the puzzle is done with your mouse. and some enemies have massive grids, and if you want them to be exposed for a reasonable length of time, you i will i be hitting every node possible. oh, and without the right hacking node? you can only expose one enemy at a time. while it works for pragmata, i honestly would ve preferred a more traditional action game. h1 side content h1 there s little else in the way of side content in this game. your shelter has spaces to print gifts for diana to play with, a training sim pod with incredibly mid challenges, and a robot brick who has a very specific currency you get by either finding it in fixed spawns or earning it from training sims. what do you get for this currency? you can use it to check off spots on a stamp board to get a paragraph of info on an enemy, info on a minor side story that took place before the events of the game, some upgrades and weapons nodes, or new outfits for diana and hugh. getting a row, three rows, and a full board cleared earns additional rewards and currency for upgrading printable weaponry. i mean, i guess it works? it s alright. in addition to the mid training sims, after beating the game, you unlock access to honestly much better challenges, which require you to completely explore one of the previous areas and defeat improved bosses to unlock. upon completion, you get upgrade materials, tokens to spend on stamp boards, new weaponry, and a new costume. spoiler and a way to unlock a secret ending... spoiler h1 final thoughts and conclusion h1 pragmata is a fun side venture from the usual resident evil stuff. it takes a concept that s been done to death and twists it in a fun way. it s worth the price!

text_cleaned: oh boy. honestly, i was kinda disappointed with the game, but i was still prepared to recommend it because i don t think it s a bad game, just...lacking. then i started writing my thoughts and ended up with three paragraphs about the disappointing parts of the combat, and it went downhill from there with the progression, the side content, the narrative...and now there s a big fat thumbs down on this review. it s more of a neutral middling thumb though, just leaning on negative because steam doesn t have a middling option. also spoiler alert. first off, yes, the combat is great. or rather, the mechanics are. between hacking, shooting, and dodging attacks, the combat is a lot more engaging than your average shooter. stacking hacking nodes to pile up damage before unleashing it all in one big burst is fun. thing is, you have to engage with all of this for every enemy, even the most minor ones, since you need to hack them one by one to reveal their weak points, and you deal piss for damage if the enemy s weak points aren t opened. it becomes a chore eventually. the hacking node that lets you open the weak spots of multiple enemies at once is one of the best simply because it cuts a lot of tedium from many of the encounters. there pretty much aren t any other approach to combat. no melee, no grenades; there are a few mines here and there, and two kinds of environmental hazards that can damage enemies in two specific areas of the game, but that s about it. other than that, it s all aim, hack, shoot. the equipment system as a whole leaves a lot to be desired. each gun has a very limited amount of ammo, and there s no ammo pickup, so you can t reload nor stock up on ammo. if you get into an encounter with low ammo for the shotgun and charged rifle i.e. the two guns that are actually good, then tough luck. your basic gun does regenerate ammo, but it does so just slowly enough to not be reliable. upgrades are boring. most gun upgrades are just incremental increases to your damage or ammo capacity. most equippable mods increase or decrease something by 10 20 ; for example, increase damage dealt by 10 at full health, increase damage dealt by 10 when close to an enemy, reduce damage taken by 20 when aiming, increase max hp by 10 , etc. etc. finding them isn t the least bit exciting. hacking nodes are also limited. if you re trying to save them, then all they do is clutter up the hacking minigame, since you want to avoid them so as to not activate and waste them. at that point, i start to think if maybe i might as well just not use any. the narrative is...well, it didn t stick with me. there s definitely a growing father daughter relationship between hugh and diana, but i honestly don t think that relationship developed naturally. as far as i can tell, they grow closer simply because the story says so. in the beginning of the game hugh explicitly mentions that he doesn t trust robots, and nothing from what i can tell shifted him away from that and towards willing to give everything for diana. is it sympathy? does he see something in her? is it just the natural paternal instinct triggered by diana s human appearance? i see a lot of people praising the narrative so it feels like i m missing something. the ending s pretty good though, that much i can say. very emotional. i suspect it would ve hit a lot harder if i was invested in the story and characters. speaking of the narrative, the story i feels i pretty short, even if it s about the same length as, say, a resident evil game. i clocked in at 11,4 hours at the end of the story. and i think that s because not much actually happens in the story itself. your objective from the very start is to stop the ai that s sending robots to kill everyone, and everything that happens in between is just roadblocks, literally and figuratively. mostly literally. so everything until then can be summarised as hugh discovers that the facility s ai has gone rogue, and allies himself with an android that he named diana. after several detours, including meeting another android, they reach the ai s tower. my last gripe is with the environments, but it s more of a nitpick than anything. i was afraid that most of the environments will be sci fi hallway, and unfortunately i was kinda right. you do go through a facsimile of new york in the second area, and it has an awesome view and a cool boss fight, but other than that it s pretty much all sci fi hallway and moon rocks. sci fi hallway with trees, sci fi hallway between moon rocks, sci fi hallway with black goop, etc. lack of variety is the game s biggest weakness as a whole. so much so that the whole game feels samey from beginning to end. you start the game hacking and blasting robots with a shotgun in a sci fi hallway, continue hacking and blasting in a sci fi hallway through the middle, and end it hacking and blasting robots with a shotgun in a sci fi hallway. it doesn t help that the game i loves i locked doors. half the obstacles you face in the game is locked doors with multiple terminals scattered around the map. even hugh comments on how many locked doors with multiple terminals there are in the game. to reiterate, the combat mechanics are great by design, and though i haven t mentioned it, the boss fights are also good, partly by virtue of not being repeated so much like other enemies. it s just that the execution of the combat mechanics makes it feel repetitive. and aside from the combat mechanics, not much about pragmata stands out; not the world, not the writing, not level design, and certainly not music which i haven t even mentioned because i genuinely have nothing to say . i can see people enjoying the game, but personally i regretted my purchase. maybe if it was on a discount i would ve been kinder, but for the full price it s going for, not worth it.

text_cleaned: so far, this game is like walking into a michelin star restaurants, sitting down and ordering the highest rated chicken dinner only to bite into it and realizing its unseasoned and dry. to be entirely honest, maybe this game just isnt for me. but the kid is not as interesting as i thought they d make it and the main character is simply kinda bland. i m not even going to mention how overdone the ai taking over space station trope is... you can t just add a kid and suddenly expect me to care about it. why should i care about this robot kid? because she has cute scenes where you can post 30 seconds on tiktok to make the game look better then it really is? is it because the hacking minigame is attached to her? is that the only thing i should need to be invested into a character? if you re looking for a genuine story about fatherhood, deep seeded moral dilemmas being tested and overcame, or a thrilling story that will have you relating and caring deeply about the characters themselves? well, if the first couple minutes are something to go by. this aint it. to be honest. i haven t played much other then the intro, but the main character is about as bland as they come. all he exists is to provide exposition and be generic father figure character. no moral boundarys explored beyond surface level. hugh simply has nothing interesting about him. i m not even talking about him just being poorly written; he just has nothing going for him. he doesn t have any interesting backstory or deeply seeded trial that he must come to terms with. he isn t particularly charming, snappy, angry, or anything other then occasionally happy. every single scene of hugh can be summed up in oh no, anyways... i simply don t know why every review keeps ignoring how boring the main characters in favor of kid is cute lol if the story and its characters continue to be this bland, i don t think i can get through it. who knows? maybe its one of those it will get better later kind of games? i ll update my review accordingly if i even make it that far. but a good game story is what i wanted and all i got instead was main character slop with a child character so generic that i couldn t be bothered to find a single moment that made me want to care about it or even a single original moment that wasn t done better in other father daughter stories . honestly feels like they just made this game to capitalize on guys who want to be father s one day and i m disappointed just how surface level the whole thing is. gameplay wise? yeah the combat is solid but not amazing by any sense, it just does alot of things; but none of them particularly well. especially considering all the mechanics they want to inject into it. think of gears of war except you only do 10 of your damage until you beat a small game of worms bejeweled on the side. but on the brightside you re given a dodge button and eventually the bejeweled game gets more icons that actually do things other then un nerf your damage.

text_cleaned: pragmata is what happens when capcom decides to go full kojima, and honestly? i m here for it. it s weird, beautiful, and surprisingly emotional. the vibe: you re an astronaut protecting a mysterious girl named diana in a world that feels like a glitchy, sci fi fever dream. the contrast between the heavy, mechanical suit and the fragile human connection is what makes this stick. the gameplay: it s not just a long escort mission. the synergy between the two characters is fluid, and the combat feels weighty and intentional. on an oled screen, the lighting and ray tracing are absolutely breathtaking the 3k clarity really makes the futuristic textures pop. the verdict: if you like games that aren t afraid to be strange and visually stunning, buy it. it s cryptic, it s polished, and it s easily one of the most unique experiences i ve played this year. 8.5 10

text_cleaned: unlike resident evil 9, which partially exists to reassure investors that capcom can keep delivering safe, controlled, by the numbers experiences for busy gamers who prefer to watch their favorite streamer reacting to the most intense moments, pragmata feels like a mistake that, by chance, ended up becoming a video game you can actually experience rather than being told about by people at the peak of the culture war who perceive it as a second revelation. still, the game isn t saved from mediocrity, with its innovation being tied to pressing an extra button, and a companion in the form of a child whose only function is to deliver tirades of cuteness and naivety for people who have never interacted with children outside of anime. the supposed father daughter relationship in the whole game is mostly in the hub and in the idle animations where diana annoys hugh the only moments where you can feel something real in their otherwise empty interactions. the rest of the time, you go through the protagonist s quirky remarks and the girl s umm actually lines. the only bright spot in the gameplay is the presence of challenge levels and red rooms, which provide genuinely interesting combat trials that at least try to fix the non existent crowd control in the main levels. the game would drastically benefit from toning down the overloaded spread of a dozen pieces of equipment that don t even let you properly experience half of what you re given from the start. while pragmata at first glance seems like a bright spot of originality, it s enough to look at who published the game to understand that they re too afraid to push players to their limits except in its audio and visual presentation. could be much worse.

text_cleaned: on paper, a set up for a really wholesome game, and it most definitely has it s moments.....i gotta be honest though, i was kinda disappointed for how highly rated it was. it s made to be a short and sweet adventure shooter game, i ll start with the positives: the visuals are amazing, can t say i m surprised, it s capcom, so it s more slightly above their bar standard if anything the combat is actually really unique! it consists of having to do a maze like puzzle game with the face buttons of your controller on every enemy to make them venerable to other kinds of damage where they would otherwise be near immune. there s some good variance to what you can build into and is by far the highlight of the game for sure. this game has a list of challenge content that you can just do at any time, and they were some of the most fun i had with the game as it highlights, the best part about the game being the fluid movement and combat. buuuuuuut that s about the most i got out of it, here are my negatives: when they said short...i wasn t expecting it to be as short as it was, i did a good amount of side content, most of it even, and the whole game took....8 hours. it really felt like it ended way too soon, like it could have had more time to tell a much more intricate story...and speaking off... the story: it s the best part about the game from what i heard from most others....but man it is....not that great. i won t spoil anything, but mister main character get almost, nothing in terms of characterization, and they backpedal on have of it that they give him! i feel like the biggest issue was it was going too fast, i felt characters wouldn t stop talking and let there be silence for like....a minute. when the game had it s moments where it slowed down, and let a scene play out naturally, it really did have some good moments, but is contradicted by a massive amount of plot holes that break the cohesion of the story in some critical ways. and the last major thing being the voice acting, it can be dry here and there, but that is mostly a story fault. this game has one of the worst conditions of repeating dialogue i have ever seen. for example, mister main character, has one line for whenever he does a finisher, one, just, one, and he says it, every time without fail. i was one take this away from pulling my hair out, and it has this problem everywhere in the game. not only that, but this game holds you hand so much on top of that, that i was getting tutorial voice lines in the final section of the game. ehhhhhhhhhhhh, was really disappointed with this one, not worth the 60 asking price and wildly overrated i think it s still a fun play if you like the sort of combat they made, but other than that, i m giving it a could have been so much better 5 10

text_cleaned: h1 tl;dr: no, that play time is not a typo. it s not a long game at all. despite that, it s still a slog with needless padding and excessive qte. h1 once we were able to acquire the game at its fair price which is in the 40s at most it was time to give it a try given good feedback at points. it feels half baked. like it wants to tell a decently good story but rushed due to how long it took to do the graphics. without spoiling: your team is exploring a station that is basically playing insert deity here , creating life from inorganic matter that can essentially create homunculi artificial beings that behave like humans. upon arrival you re beset upon by walking automatons and a computerized voice ordering your destruction. two of your crew are removed from the premises in a very tropey way, like a b tier movie. the other straight out of a bad horror flick ends up separated from you, then you hear their blood curdling scream as the voice has ordered their annihilation. you see what was written there? chances are you ve seen some unscary film from the 90s that played out very similar. that s what you have here: a script that plays out like one of those terrible cult films, all the way to the normal ending. to say more would effectively spoil a lot; suffice to say, your enjoyment of the game circles around whether you can suspend your belief at what s going on long enough to learn what happened. it s a tragic tale for sure, but a lot goes unexplained in the cutscenes. there may have been some random reading somewhere that explained it but we expect the game to explain it in the cutscenes . during the learning of events you ll walk through various tunnels that follow a very cut and paste sequence: you open a door, there s some enemies that attack you, you go through another door, there s something that blocks you, you go through another door, you see something behind some glass that you have to then walk the long way around to find, etc etc. it got old. this segment felt very close to crisis core: final fantasy vii, where as you navigate a path you just hit these clearances where you know there ll be a fight. much was made of the hacking in order to deal larger damage than what is normally available. while creative, it also got old, and we weren t fans of the two situations where you re forced to do it. it makes sense in the context of the characters, but it felt needlessly burdensome; somewhat similar to forspoken s final sequences. one area is a badly generated downtown cityscape that looked quite nice; but was limited in its exploration. graphics are almost never an issue in the game; it s a nice looking game, certainly. music was also decent though repetitive . so the story was decent, the graphics were good, the music was decent and the battle engine was decent. why then do we not recommend it? because there simply isn t enough here to justify anything more than 40 at a stretch. even with all of the various items to find, even with the training stuff, even with learning about the world, it s just not enough of a game. not when clair obscur which is arguably 4 5 times as long with a stronger story debuted at less than 50. we didn t really like obscur that much, but we recognize it s superior to this in almost every way except graphics.

text_cleaned: a lovely game from start to finish with a solid story, super unique and extremely fun gameplay, a ton of build variety, and really rich characters. a very decent length not being too long or too short, and i would say it s fully worth the price. while the story is nothing crazy, its heartfelt and keeps you on the line across the entire game while funneling you a quite frankly enormous amount of interactions between hugh and diana. it feels like they always have something new to say and talk about, and expand each other s lore in non intrusive and natural ways that really make you connect with them. the dub performance is great, although i feel that the dialogue direction wasn t the best. many of the main story conversations felt very scriptwritten rather than something natural the characters would say in that situation. still, the voice acting is genuinely very skilled to the point that this isn t a major issue. the gameplay is incredible, combining a one of a kind form of shooting and puzzle gameplay in a way that just completely makes sense and naturally clicks from the moment you try it, despite how strange it sounds at first. the gunplay is very similar to ratchet and clank or dead space, in that its less about standard everyday guns and instead more about an extremely varied and fun arsenal that can be combined in a near endless amount of ways for some really rewarding gameplay. the hacking also doesn t take a backseat, and is just as important as your weapons, the build variety even allows you to focus on one or the other, or create some sick synergies between both. backing this gameplay is a very solid roster of enemy types, giving you a lot to keep a track of and respond to in combat. i had no issues in regards to performance and i encountered very few bugs and just one that was gamebreaking stuck under map once doing a finisher animation on a ramp . that said, i must say the path tracing implementation was not done well. even as someone who was able to run cyberpunk 2077 with pt and all settings maxed out at 90fps, pragmata s pt was a massive hit, taking me under 60 frames for a very barely noticeable change in lighting from rt. i recommend playing with pt off if you re struggling for frames, even if you have it on the difference is negligible. all in all, a great new ip from capcom that shows they ve still got what it takes to hit the industry with something new and innovative.

text_cleaned: i get why people like pragmata. the premise hooked me immediately too, something goes wrong on a moon base, you are sent to investigate, and everything falls apart. it sounds exactly like doom 3, which i love. on top of that, you are paired with a small girl you need to protect, which clearly aims for that last of us emotinal angle. sounds like perfect combo. but honestly, it doesn t land for me. the atmosphere just isn t there. the intro sets a serious tone but the main character feels way too casual. one minute your whole team is gone, the next you are chilling and playing with toys like nothing happened. it completely breaks the immersion. the bond with the girl also feels forced. the game keeps pushing cute moments instead of letting that connection build naturally, which feels cringe. gameplay wise, it is pretty underwhelming so far. it amost feels like i am playing candy crush in a sci fi setting. the visuals are great but the combat lacks tension. it never feels dangerous or dark. unfortunately, i will be refunding for now. it feels overpriced for what it delivers. i might give it another shot later on a big discount.

text_cleaned: ok the question is, is this game worth the 50 asking price at release, my answer is yes. it s fun, it runs well, the combat is interesting, the movement feels good, the visual design is great and the overall story is compelling enough to keep you going. it s a good game for 50. is it a dad game? no, the story and relationship between hugh and diana just appears, there s no character devolpment, there isn t any time spent where hugh is standoffish or begrudges diana s presence, he never views her as a tool, as soon as they meet hugh is just 100 on board with being a dad and that carries all the way to the end of the story. this is basically a resident evil story, it s mid tier at best, it exists as a reason to be places and do things rather than a driving force, a story where people exist as plot devices rather than enities with their own goals. what i m saying is this, will you get a game you ll enjoy? sure. will you still be talking about it in 6 months? no.

text_cleaned: after trying the demo, i knew i had to play the full game and what a blast it turned out to be. i just rolled credits at around 13 hours and have already unlocked the post game content and ng which i can t wait to dive into . this has easily been one of the most fun aaa experiences i ve had in recent years. the story itself isn t groundbreaking, but the way it s delivered works well. the dynamic between the main characters is definitely the highlight. there s a subtle emotional core that builds over time and gives real weight to your actions. it never feels overly sentimental, but it s enough to keep you invested beyond just what happens next. the voice acting was also a pleasant surprise, especially diana. her mannerisms and interactions are genuinely fun to watch. visually, the game is stunning. the sterile aesthetic fits the sci fi setting perfectly, and it s one of the most beautiful games i ve played, made even better by how smoothly it runs and i didn t encounter any bugs, which honestly feels rare these days. the biggest innovation, though, is the gameplay. the mix of hacking puzzles and shooting might seem odd at first glance, but the execution is excellent. from start to finish, it never felt like it got in the way. in fact, it consistently enhanced the combat encounters and the amount of combinations you have with weapons, mods and hacks can change the flow of combat quite a bit. honestly, this is one of the biggest surprises of 2026 for me. i ll be shocked if it doesn t end up in my top 5 by the end of the year.

text_cleaned: more than a game a heartbreakingly beautiful journey. i bought pragmata thinking i d be playing a sci fi survival action game. i ended up experiencing a masterpiece that redefined what emotional storytelling in games can be. capcom s latest ip is often called a dad game, but it feels much deeper than that. it s a story about connection in its purest form. you play as hugh, a system auditor on a desolate lunar station, but your whole world quickly starts to revolve around diana a little android girl with the heart of a human child. the gameplay loop is brilliant. the way you have to protect diana while she hacks your enemies creates a bond that i haven t felt since the best moments of the last of us or resident evil 4. but the real magic happens in the shelter. giving her gifts, watching her curiosity grow, and just having these quiet, soulful conversations about what earth was like... it s incredibly moving. it makes you feel like a protector, a teacher, and a father all at once. technically, the game is a marvel the re engine detail is insane! , but its real strength is its sincerity. it doesn t try to be edgy or cynical. it s optimistic, warm, and brave. it s a short but perfect experience that stays with you long after the credits roll. please, do yourself a favor and meet diana. you won t regret it. verdict: 10 10. essential. a soul searching experience in a digital world.

text_cleaned: its hard to identify a favorite thing about pragmata because the gameplay is awesome and the story is cool as , but the level design is insane! it takes aspect from dark souls in the way that checkpoints function, but checkpoints take you back to your home base instead of just sitting down. this forces more interaction with diana the main npc because the exit is across the shelter from the entrance, further engaging the player with diana. the actual level design is also incredibly thought out. there s an argument to be made about how much yellow paint blue in this game exists... but there are tons of hidden areas which aren t called out by traditional eye catchers or blue paint tarp etc... . many of the hidden things don t quite feel hidden, but that is because the level design naturally draws your eyes to them, and usually not in a meta gamey i m a gamer i look for these kind of way. the level design also just flows so well. i ve missed secrets that i noticed on first observation but by the time i had made it to the area i could get to the secret, i had forgotten about it and the level design just kept drawing me further. the game feels very open world even though it is very linear this is a positive! linear games are not bad! all the guns and hacking nodes combine into an insane number of viable builds and while i have my own preferences, the system feels fairly dedicated to the play your own way idea. everything feels very balanced and you don t necessarily get locked into any one playstyle, the game is designed for you to roll with the loot lying around once you start running out of hacks ammo that you brought with you. but you absolutely can and in my opinion should specialize, you may just need to visit the shelter more often to restock. the only hard limit on your upgrade options is pure lunum, and for the things you get to upgrade craft with it, i get it. spend pure lunum on your main kit you leave the shelter with, but make sure to upgrade other stuff at least a little for when you get stuck in a corner and that s all that is lying around.

text_cleaned: visually stunning is the only positive thing i can say about this game. the environments? beautiful. everything else? uninteresting and forgettable. if you go in for challenging gameplay? this game is extremely casual, with barely any challenges. the boss fights are the only dynamic spikes. the fights against the mobs get boring real fast. i never felt any pressure or any danger, from any encounter. and if you go in for a story? this game i has i no story. please understand: i am a serial enjoyer who approaches any game with an open mind, no cynicism, trying to meet it at its terms, and trying my best to enjoy what it gives. i don t like to hate watch shows or hate play games. and i am also ready to cry over a good tear jerker moment handed to me at the drop of a hat. and this game has left me absolutely indifferent and apathetic. the gaming landscape right now is teeming with multi layered stories that delve into its heroes inner worlds; stories that double as metaphors for trauma; stories that examine big societal questions and stand in conversation with big moral quandaries... i pragmata i has no point to its story and says absolutely nothing. it s not about grimdark vs. wholesome either. a game can be wholesome and still say something meaningful. i pragmata i just doesn t. it has two flat two dimensional stock characters, who go through completely standard unimaginative predictable story steps that you have seen a thousand times before. everything about its plot you can see from a mile away. it has no humor. it has no heart. and the relationship between hugh and diana has no emotional depth, no single point of anchor, no stakes, no progression. they are as bland and uninspired as the story they inhabit. so you are left with this negative feedback loop: one may forgive repetitive gameplay for a plot that elevates the experience. this game doesn t have that. one may hand waive a paper thin plot when it s clearly only there as stage setting for interesting gameplay. this game doesn t have that either. now, capcom knows how to make good games. i am a fan of resident evil, for one. and while i did not think requiem was their best work in the franchise, it was still a very solid experience. this was just embarrassing. and the true ending was just lazy. spoiler we see more and more games now that roll credits on you, and then ask you to examine the world more closely. you see it in roguelites, or spiralling narratives where you cannot trust the world that you see. i pragmata i is just a stock standard cinematic space adventure. it warrants no such addition. and it cannot build on itself. instead, what you get is a room with simulation challenges with upscaled difficulty. i 100 d every area before finishing the game, so i only had to go back and fight the uptuned bosses to unlock the rest. so, in the end, all this is is a time sink with an uptick in difficulty that didn t bother me but will, i suspect, frustrate more casual players. and all it does, aside from some cosmetic rewards, is it gives you an unexplained mcguffin that can miraculously prevent hugh from dying to the genre appropriate infection he has contracted. which, i must add, changes nothing about the final game cutscene. you go back, you beat the final boss again, and you get the same scene as before, the same conversation, and the same implication that he dies in the end. except after all the credits and cutscenes, you get one extra one, which is just a black screen with a voiceover from a bot implying that he actually survived. it is so hilariously lazy and, frankly, devalues what little emotional weight this story might have had i honestly have to assume the ending you see in the game is the ending they intended, and then the upper management just decided to append bonus hard content somehow and threw in this nothingburger thing there, too, making the one meaningful scene in the game utterly meaningless. spoiler i do not like leaving negative reviews. but the fact that this game is at overwhelmingly positive boggles my mind, because it s such a banal mid experience.
