text_cleaned: pragmata has completely won me over so far. one of the biggest reasons is that it feels like the kind of bold, original game we used to get more often in the 90s and 2000s. it takes real creative swings, and i have missed that. so many games today feel overly safe or familiar, but this one actually feels fresh. the gameplay grabbed me right away because it is doing its own thing, and it works. i am solving puzzles to hack enemies while aiming, firing, and dodging at the same time, and somehow it all feels natural. what has surprised me even more is how much the story and the dynamic between the characters are resonating with me personally. i recently became a dad to a daughter, so this game is hitting me in a way it probably would not have a few years ago. it is giving the experience more emotional weight than i expected. i think the story and the relationship between the characters have enough warmth to connect with anybody, whether they are a parent or not, but for me, it has felt especially meaningful. i have not finished it yet, so i cannot speak to how everything comes together in the end, but as of now i am loving it. it feels creative, memorable, and personal in a way that really stands out. highly recommended so far.

text_cleaned: this review is 100 spoiler free. tl;dr: this game does most things well, but it s fairly short and has limited replay value. good gameplay, excellent story, good overall, but not for 60. get it on sale if you can wait that long, but avoiding story spoilers is paramount. story: the story is 100 narrative feels. you have no agency, you are merely a chariot carrying the plot forward. it is railroaded, emotionally evocative, and designed to make you care about what happens to the two main characters while painting a bleak picture of the damaging effects of pride, greed, loss, and tragedy in between rays of hope and moments of happiness. it does this very well. gameplay: the hacking shooting combo mechanics are new, interesting, and well executed. the two main characters must rely on each other s strengths to overcome obstacles and achieve their goals. the interplay between the two make for very satisfying combos. mastering fast paced sequences of hacking, dodge timing, and a quick aim are well rewarded. flow: the main loop has you accessing the entire game s content via a destination selector on a tram pod, straight into a loading screen, and then they drop you into the level. it s a paced sequential mix of exploration, puzzle solving, combat, and story progression. it is entirely non contiguous, and seems broken up in this way to keep hardware requirements low, and platform portability high. there are several control snatching cutscenes and gameplay interrupting info windows though. one info window that got me killed. popped up during a jump and cancelled inputs, dropping me to my death progression: weapons, upgrades, abilities, mods, and unlocks are found earned via level drops and currencies: 1 common for basic weapon ability upgrades, 1 rare for certain threshold tiers on those items, and 1 to spend on character stats in 3 basic categories: plot chariot hp def, character 1 primary ability, character 2 primary ability. fairly standard stuff. completing the last objective of each major area increases the upper limits of your upgradeable weapons and abilities, and some unlock new weapons and abilities. scattered throughout each level are hideout decorations and training sims data side content . finding and placing the hideout decorations rewards a stamp for a bingo card. these cards reward skins, lore, red door keys, survivability upgrades, and sometimes mods abilities. any red door key opens any red door. risk and reward await behind each, but you cannot open them all without doing the side content. side content: the side content is uninspired at best: virtualize an obstacle course, meet criteria, get rewards. they are poorly tuned, and poorly designed, but they make up the bulk of the content by area count: 5 times the number of these as main story levels. some are trivial, almost effortless. some are infuriatingly difficult requiring tight timings in janky situations. they are wholly optional, but the lack of a scoreboard, ranking system, and the insignificance of the rewards make this a dull and easily skippable grind. environment art: they seemed to lean into 4 environmental storytelling themes here: high tech large scale 3d printing, 3d printing gone wrong, seismic debris damage, and 3d printing gone horrifically wrong. it fits, thematically, but there s not enough hand painted detail object placement for such a small explorable area. maybe this was done for performance reasons? level design: a lot of safe knowns here. puzzles, platforming, combat, exploration, hidden rewards. nothing new, nothing fancy. unfortunately, they are all tiny, and there are only 6 of them. it is also a bit illogical that despite the clear degradation of the environment due to circumstances, all the baddie printers seem to work just fine. performance: actually good while looking good. i m not super savvy in this area, but the engine and environment seem well optimized. no stuttering, higher framerates on higher settings with better visual quality than anything on ue5. but that s a low bar, and the game environments being tiny and lacking detail is probably helping performance quite a lot here. but most people who buy this game don t care about any of that. we re here to get shot right in the feels out of nowhere by a 7 year old girl who quickly becomes the only thing in the whole world that matters. we re here to be the kind of dad a lot of us can only wish we had, or hope we become some day. we re here to protect her, no matter the cost, because that s what good dads do.

text_cleaned: h1 the moon s best kept secret? why capcom s pragmata is a sci fi experiment worth trying h1 the dual control system between hugh and diana in capcom s pragmata transforms potential escort mission tropes into a rewarding, synchronized action puzzler reminiscent of i the world ends with you i . this core mechanic requires precise, simultaneous management of both characters to deliver satisfying combat synergy. h3 pros h3 synergy between hugh and diana that feels incredibly rewarding. training room pushes your reflexes and think outside of the box. gorgeous lunar aesthetic that only capcom s re engine could pull off this well. the upgrade loop feels addicting, replaying in new game plus mode feels like a new experience. you ll likely finish the whole game in one sitting or a weekend due to the story pacing. h3 con h3 steep learning curve initially when managing two characters at once. h2 a dual threat system that actually works h2 let s be real: when capcom first teased pragmata, we all thought it was just going to be another escort mission game. the dual control system between hugh and diana is the beating heart of this experience, and it s surprisingly reminiscent of i the world ends with you i . you aren t just protecting diana; you are operating as a single, lethal unit. hugh handles the heavy lifting as the gunner, but he s essentially firing blanks unless diana is doing her thing. playing as diana requires you to hack through enemy defenses via rhythmic, precise button inputs. once she hacks through the enemy, hugh can lock on and deliver the killing blow. this combat synergy is satisfying in a way few action games manage to achieve. it s not just about button mashing; it s about a synchronized dance between hugh and diana. h2 the grind is the goal h2 i ll admit, the story is shorter than i expected given the price tag. but capcom clearly designed this with high replayability in mind. the game practically begs you to dive back into previous areas to hunt for resources and aim for completion. the upgrade system for skills and attributes is deep enough that you really feel the difference between a base level hugh and a fully kitted out hunter. every time you revisit a sector, you re faster, stronger, and more efficient. h2 pushing your limits in the training room h2 pragmata s training room is designed to test your limits through its pre equipped hacking skills and weapons. playing on a blind playthrough can feel repetitive, if you re aiming for a perfect run on your first try to get the most of the rewards. given that most of the training rooms have its own set of time limits, efficiency is the key to mastery. one of the training rooms stood out because one objective required me to avoid an invisible enemy entirely. rushing in without paying attention meant instant failure. i had devise a strategy to use the provided defensive equipment and hack through the invisible enemy. another training room made me use a different approach. instead of taking enemies down myself, i had to make them attack each other by hacking one of the support drones. my usual approach didn t work, so i had to pay attention to how each enemy behaved and time my hacks properly. h2 a cinematic sprint to the finish h2 the lunar surface feels immersive and as someone who loves space themed stories, it feels like home to me. the narrative pacing of the story felt brief without fillers. the game felt bittersweet for me. the emotional parts of the story between hugh and diana s relationship sticks with you. h2 conclusion h2 pragmata as a space enthusiast is enjoyable as a whole as long as you manage your expectations given the price point. if you can get past the initial steep learning curve of the game s intro, you ll find an action puzzler with innovation.

text_cleaned: one thing this game has, something many aaa titles lack, is personality. that s what stuck with me the most. yes i ve seen one of the reviews state this as well, i completely agree with it at its core, the game builds a father daughter like relationship between hugh and the android pragmata diana. early on, it didn t really click for me. but over time, the small interactions, diana relying on hugh, the way he protects her without hesitation sure start to feel natural. by the later stages, you can genuinely feel that bond. it s surprisingly wholesome. even as someone who doesn t want kids, this felt like a really grounded and true connection. now, the action. the combat definitely has a learning curve. juggling hacking sequences while dodging incoming attacks and lining up shots can get hectic fast, especially during boss fights where timing matters a lot more than you d expect. there were moments where i got overwhelmed trying to multitask and just got one shot due to low health, or being overwhelmed by walkers other enemies.. i didn t beat a single boss on my first try i do play on the standard difficulty and yeah, that was a bit frustrating. but it never felt unfair. once it clicks, the flow of combat actually feels really satisfying. everything around it, the level design, pacing, and presentation truly feels like classic capcom quality. the ending hit harder than i expected. it s both beautiful and sad, and yeah i even shed a small tear. i can definitely recommend this game. on to the post game content and probably a new game run right after. 9 10

text_cleaned: pragmata is a remarkable game. a game that essentially was a tech demo that could have just faded away and be forgotten capcom instead delayed and cooked it to make an amazing game instead. they even managed to come up with some novel gameplay mechanics that haven t really been done before. if you re unfamiliar the game is like a hybrid action puzzle game. in order to damage the enemies you have to hack them with the girl, diana, to weaken them. then you use your weapons as the main character hugh to finish them off. as the game progresses you quickly find out that the puzzles aren t just for the enemies, a lot of encounters are almost puzzles themselves which weapon to use, which hack to use, and on which enemy to use them, etc. it s really interesting and fun. the combat is smooth and responsive, certainly one of the better playing capcom games. it s feels closer to something like a dmc than a resident evil. the game has several different areas to explore, and it s somewhat open to where you can go, not necessarily a linear path, and there are some metroidvania elements at play to get you to return to some areas if you want to find extra goodies. the game is filled with a lot of extra stuff like that too, costumes to unlock, there are essentially a bunch of vr missions think something like the early mgs games , that net you extra rewards, level up materials and such. i didn t get to everything in the game my first time though, but i did play a good chunk and it took me around 18 hours to finish the game. the game even offers up some extras after you finish it so it gives you incentive to play even more. overall an outstanding game. great characters, story, gameplay, graphics, capcom is hitting on all cylinders here.

feel like i m the target audience for this game being a guy in my mid 30 s but i couldn t understand these characters let alone relate to them, the only person i felt sorry for in the story was someone you never even meet which made all these emotional moments fall flat. there were some weird inconsistencies with the story too where you speak about the quake which killed your colleagues or maybe not 2 of them flew into space, don t know what happened to them yet later they talk about the quake as something which was a part of the personnel on the station dying like it happened long before you arrived. some of the enemy designs are pretty alright, but others feel quite lazy. when you expose an enemy it s nice to see how their internals make sense and weak points become exposed, until you look at some where these weak points to poop out of nothing for seemingly no reason. the enemies become tedious when you are still fighting the first introductory enemy to the game in the final chapter with the most interesting enemy only appearing twice during the entire story. most melee enemies pose no threat at all as you can just dodge back, hack and shoot them very easily. some of the ranged enemies like the gunner are more annoying to deal with and were almost the only time apart from bosses where i found myself wanting to use anything apart from the pistol. i did notice some quite jank hitboxes too on the melee enemies especially a certain corrupted boss. i think i only died 2 times during the run, once was quite early when i was having issues getting used to switching between hacking and shooting and got caught out, the second was right near the end in the final red zone because i got a bit dodge happy and ran out of stamina, but i can also count the number of times i healed using the recover items on 1 hand so maybe that would have saved one of those deaths if i used more. during my 13 hour run i got 100 of everything i could before post game, perfected every trial, 100 d each zone, collected all the mini cabins. i don t want to complain about the length of the game but if you just focus on the story it will probably take 6 7 hours for a somewhat competent gamer to get through, if they added even just 1 more hour to get some more character progression in it would have helped a lot. the final boss left a very bitter taste in my mouth, who doesn t love when they decide to make the final climactic boss a qte? i really don t understand why this is still a thing in games, but it killed off any desire to want to mess around with any of the post game stuff or attempted ng on lunatic mode.

text_cleaned: incredible game with a unique combat system that combines some of the dynamism of maneuver shooters with the cerebral satisfaction of puzzle solving. the puzzles on their own are very simple so the game demands you do them mid combat without any pause. it makes combat feel very fast paced. you feel like a genius when you master the flow of physical combat and hacking. on gameplay alone i d say this is a must play. the story is beautiful. if all you want to do is fight and beat the game then you re more than welcome to do that. if you choose to engage with the story, collect the rems, and talk with diana regularly, however, then you ll connect much better with the game. i laughed and cried, and i don t think i would have enjoyed my time quite as much if i didn t immerse myself in the smaller, sweeter story of hugh and diana s relationship alongside the greater one happening around me. i really only have one small gripe in that mid air thrusting feels a little clunky, but it s manageable and it didn t significantly impact my experience. i highly recommend this one.

text_cleaned: i just finished this game last night. i cried a lot. the game itself is excellent, i love the flow of the shooting with the puzzle hacking, i like all the different mechanics except the spoiler jamming antennas spoiler , i hate those , i like how older mechanics get spruced up in later parts of the game, i like the overall difficulty curve, etc. etc. it s the most fun i ve had with a third person shooter in years, and it might be one of the best re engine games to date. this doesn t even touch on the obvious familial bond you develop in the eyes of hugh while you interact with diana. there is a bit of dissonance at the beginning if you interface with the shelter s free talk system too much too early, as hugh has an initially somewhat distant attitude towards her that lasts until after the first boss fight, and that distance isn t accounted for in some of those conversations. aside from that, if you take breaks from the main stages or the game itself often enough and interact with the collectables, you really do develop a fairly strong attachment towards diana as she learns about herself, humans, and hugh. there are also a good number of optional chatter conversations that can occur as you re explore past stages. i really, really want a pragmata 2, or maybe a story expansion dlc. there s a lot of narrative plot holes that will need to be addressed in such a release, but i really want to see where this ip goes if it doesn t take a more anthology style approach . but more than anything else, i want to explore what happens after the game s ending; where will things go from here? goty 2026 contender for me, definitely. i ll treasure this game forever.

text_cleaned: this game is an unexpected surprise but a sure welcome one, i haven t finished it but i do have a lot to say: let s start off by addressing the combat, from reviews i see a lot of praise that i agree with, but from my close circles people are generally very against it for some reason, even though i personally see it as finally! new original game mechanic! a unique twist on combat and isn t boring or repetitive and changes a lot throughout the game!! while they see it as wtf is this, i cant focus on both at the same time, dumb character, doesnt feel good!! which is a statement that i heavily disagree with. having played the demo before the game out i enjoyed the combat to a degree, i thought it was too easy, but with the main game it certainly gets more challenging and has dedicated challenge runs for you to test out your skills, the movement is fluid and responsive which i appreciate a lot, the game is well optimized and combat generally feels smooth. a complaint about the combat is that diana often repeats herself a lot which gets annoying after a while which i get handholding players on the lower side of the iq chart so it doesn t bother me much. the visuals are stunning, hugh s design reminds me of titanfall and that is amazing. the diana dynamic is one of the best parent games i have seen, i compare it to god of war 2018, the worldbuilding with how hugh teaches diana about earth is so awesome, and i could listen to the dialogue for hours. overall 8.5 10, can t wait to beat the game, high five 33

text_cleaned: an incredbily uninteresting and dull experience on all levels. the gameplay and the story borrows from great games without delivering anything fresh. the only unique thing about the game is the implementation of hacking, which can be skipped with the auto hacking mod that is unlocked in the middle of the game. when the enemies respawn it makes a bit of sense, since they are 3d printed on the spot. when hugh respawns it doesn t make any sense. the gameplay is too repetitive to support even 10 hours of gameplay, the routine get s old too quickly. turns out that simply replacing horror elements and tension with a clean aestetic does not make for a good experience. this kind of gameplay works when resources are limited and the enemies can be avoided, but pragmata is mostly a series of small combat arenas, where hugh can run around enemies like it s nothing with infinite ammo, where even shooting a lot is optional. the story also doesn t save this experience at all. it is shocking that in 2026 a parent and child dynamic in a video game, that has been done to masterful degree in games going back more than 10 years ago completely fails to deliver a single moment that does not come off as totally fake and flat.

text_cleaned: at the time of this review i had just beat the game. the story: the story isn t hard to follow, capcom love sprinkling details about the goings on in the world throughout the course of the game which is always fun to investigate and find out, the characters bounce off each other perfectly well, nothing feels inorganic, 100 natural. 10 10. the setting: semi modern space aesthetics are something i love basically starfield is starfield didn t suck and this game delivers it rather well, the main character, hugh, his suit looks really cool, very much something you can see appear within the next 70 years or so. the main location of the game looks nice, even the messier parts aren t a bad to look at, some areas have a sort of cameron aliens vibe to it which is nice. gameplay: the combat is fun, really fun, without giving spoilers, i felt the challenge incline throughout the course of the game, only 2 enemies in this game i had a personal gripe with, but don t let that spoil the game for you, the enemies are pretty fun to fight and you ve got an amazing assortment of options to deal with said enemies in various ways that cater to different playstyles. 10 10 music: the music is alright, only a couple tracks seem memorable to me, but the credits song is very nice, highly recommend staying for the credits just for that song. 6 10 optimization: denuvo...oh how i loathe denuvo, but inspite denuvo s addition to the game, the game works really damn well on high settings. and getting minimum 100 to 144 fps. 10 10 so yeah, capcom came up with a new ip and more often than not, they delivered quality work, i really hope the devs were paid well for this or given a big bonus because this game is an absolute treat. overall 10 10 was going to say 9 10 but the game s optimization brought it up to a 10 10 for me.

text_cleaned: first off, pragmata is absolutely stunning. it s a masterclass in sci fi space visuals, aesthetic sound design, and intuitive space suit design. whether it s the music, the acting, or the environment, pragmata is such a treat. diana is adorable, and hugh is a class act as an adult. however, that s where all the praise stops and the flags get raised on many other issues with the game. pragmata feels like huge potential that s been completely overshot with corrections and what feels like a complete rewrite, along with a gameplay that feels redundant and never challenging. the game is roughly 15 hours with 100 map cleared, then the unknown signal post game is another 90 120 minutes yielding the true ending which is just painfully disappointing no spoilers on the content of the ending but spoiler it s two lines, on black screen. spoiler . there s something incredibly frustrating about the writing in this game. in the moments opening the game hugh is with his crew, his captain, and what seems like his best friends. they re cast to the side almost immediately, and hugh makes no effort to ever talk about them outside of maybe 2 extremely short interactions. spoiler at no point does it seem like he grieves, or mourns for their deaths. just a passing comment about how people can not be replaced. spoiler hugh seems like he was originally written to not have a passing interest in, or having children. diana in her initial trailers was much more reserved, distant, and yet shy to express herself or embarrassed to have to delay the game for 4 years . yet, in pragmata hugh is instant father within 20 minutes. throwing down the cliche be careful, if something happened to you i wouldn t know what i d instantly. diana is a completely perfect accurate child. a pragmata with no prior memories is immediately capable of understanding the psychology of a 7 year old child when it s explained to her by hugh. as you play the game more and more, there are points in the middle and during key scenes where diana clearly seems to be grasping being a child more and more. it s beautiful, it s touching and admirable to see her become more human . but in the same hand, immediately ruined by how easily she gets it , without question or struggle to understand. diana just understands what s true and not for how typical 7 year old children act. hugh is absolutely perfect to diana and always has the right answers, and this is at times frustrating. he never shows visible agitation, or becomes vulnerable in front of diana. it s just so incredibly frustrating to see a character who s never been anything close to a father figure, just never show weakness, a lack of understanding or even a moment of vulnerability. i wish this review could ve been a recommend, but for 15 hours, 70 deluxe edition game. i can t in full faith say this is a game i can offer a 97 recommendation rate. grab it on sale for like 25 off, it ll be a decent price point by then.

text_cleaned: very highly recommended. this is, in almost all respects, a very good game. right out the door it has phenomenal art, music, the movement is fun, the weapons are all useable, the controls are good even while multi tasking during a fight, and just overall really great. is it perfect? no. so i want to get the biggest issue i have that s an affront that the game should address: how dare you not give us a pat action during conversations to pat diana on the head! she s a good potato and deserves hugs or head pats. and the next issue... why can t we play with her? we should be able to play. why can t we sit and watch tv with her? or draw? or play in the sand box? we can t even sit next to the tent and roast a marshmallow? seriously capcom, do you even want people to play this game? ugh. now, the largest and most offensive things out of the way we can look at the slightly lesser issues: the fight in the shopping district with that giant, giant, boss that keeps looking your way. : that boss can be a bit bothersome with trying to pop the reds and i kind of wish it was a touch smaller overall. not a huge, huge, issue but it was an annoyance. air dash could use a few more bursts, slightly longer in the air. stamina wasn t so bad in fights, but trying to reach places got tricky sometimes. i also wish we had more ammo for all the weapons, but luckily they were plentifully found when it mattered. some of the mobs though would have made extra ammo helpful since they are such sponges. oh, and before i forget! i really wish we had a better map. like an actual, factual, map in game. he has that hud that he pulls open on his wrist, why can t we get a map? you mean to tell me he was sent to explore and fix a space station and wasn t provided any way to know where he is, or where he has been? this right here is an actual annoyance as i got lost in a few levels multiple times. and that s honestly about the end of the bad points. the rest is almost universally positive. it s a really fun game, the traversal doesn t feel bad at all, the button config was done pretty well so i was able to fight and hack at the same time due to the different buttons. i actually appreciated this as it didn t slow the game down or freeze for a mini game, it felt more like we were in an actual fight and just trying to multi task. the characters were good and i really liked hugh, and diana was adorable. going to be honest with this part: it felt really good to play an actual, actual, good man. hugh is a good man. so many games in the last decade or so have felt it needed that the mc be some snarky, bordering on evil, jerk. we don t tell her to be quiet, we don t snap, we don t have a choice that says you did a good job and he responds with you really should have tried harder, but i guess you did a good job for a failed piece of tech. or some other bs. a straight up good person was just so refreshing. that alone was worth the buy.

text_cleaned: a wonderful game that captured the 5 second loop of fun consistently throughout its gameplay. pragmata is a third person shooter puzzle game metroidvania game with some souls like mechanics, taking the best of every genre and putting into one complete and nicely presented package. the gameplay loop involve the android girl, diana, first hacking the enemies through a simple and quick puzzle game so that hugh, the spacemarine with a heart of gold who is a better father than he probably thought he was can shoot the vulnerable enemy within the time that they are open, rinse and repeat until they are dead. that is the loop. more enemies are introduced, with more mechanics that the player will have to grapple with, but that is the basic loop of the battle in the game and it is infinitely fun. traversal and exploration also adds into the fun of the game, as player can solve environmental puzzles and overcome challenges to pick up collectibles and resources to become stronger. the shelter is the place where the player converts the collectibles into strength. it is a hub that the player can access through escape hatches that acts like bonfire, it is a homebase where you can build a playground for diana while doing upgrades and overcoming challenges to become stronger. but every time the player uses an escape hatch, all the enemies in the stage respawn, just like in a soul game, allowing the player to grind if they wish. bossfights, though limited in number, introduce their own challenges and mechanics, tells and playstyle to overcome them. none are frustratingly hard, but they are not easy either, so be prepared to die a few times before the player get the pattern on them right. additionally, the game is beautiful, with all the stages having their own identity and unique gimmicks, while also being concise enough to not get boring as the player navigates through them. the story is basic, you can probably guess most of what is going to happen before it happens, but it is presented so well and the characters, as few as there are, are so lovable that it elevates the experience to something that is unforgettable. pragmata is a highly recommended game that needs to be played. it s short. it s concise. it s packed full of fun. i have never jumped onto a higher difficulty so fast after finishing a game. great game.

text_cleaned: game developer review i work professionally as a game developer and have done for over 12 years. working on projects such as path of exile 1 2 and icarus as a conceptual designer. i wanted to leave a review of this game from a developer standpoint in hopes that it helps anyone looking to purchase pragmata. keep in mind this is an early game review. visuals 10 10 visually the game is stunning. while they haven t reinvented the wheel in terms of art style, what they have done is polish and develop existing seen before styles into something extremely appealing. character designs are strong as are weapon and prop designs. the animation work flows beautifully while also feeling impactful during combat. the environmental work is immersive and vast, this is where the game really thrives art wise. mood is felt whether playing in interior or exterior settings, it s honestly the most unique part of the game from an art standpoint. really great work. gameplay 7.5 10 nothing much new in terms of gameplay. the base mechanics of the game is basically aim, shoot, dodge, jump, which is fine. however, the hacking system is very unique and frankly very enjoyable. i m still very early game so i m enjoying that system so far, it looks like the way you can build your character around that system is very cool and offers a lot of options for unique builds suited to your style of play. my only hesitation regarding it so far is that it feels like you need to hack almost every enemy to be effective, which may become repetitive in later game. but as i said, i m still in the early game and if they build on those mechanics going forward it looks very promising. outside of combat the environment is fun to traverse. plenty of unique areas that are fun to explore along with a lot of hidden items. the game offers puzzles that need solving to get to new areas. this can easily be an area that most games fall short, becoming annoying for players when puzzle are unclear. not so in pragmata. i m enjoying solving environmental puzzles and going back to earlier areas to explore further after equipping upgrades that allow me to do so. story 8 10 again, they haven t reinvented the wheel here, at least so far, but the story is engaging and interesting. covering subjects that re controversial in current day society i m excited to see where it leads. diana is appealing and from very early on you feel protective over her. she almost boarders on being a little bit too weeby without crossing the line, which is good, instead of being annoyed by her she offers comic relief and emotional ties to the narrative. hugh is the vessel that you play through and doesn t offer a lot of personality as such, but i think that s intended and actually a good thing. you learn about the world as he does, if he d been to vibrant from a personality standpoint it may grind the wrong way against players trying to engage and be immersed by the world of the game. along with that the story in general is straight up interesting, i m wanting to push further into the game not just because of gameplay upgrades but also to find out more of the narrarive. overall 8.5 so far this game offers a lot of promise. it lays a strong foundation for a good game, even a great game if they continue to build upon the current mechanics in a unique way. i could easily see this game moving up to a 9 or 9.5. so fingers crossed it doesn t fall apart. i d recommend picking it up and giving it a go if it looks like your kind of game and genre. if you re hesitant, no worries, i d strongly suggest this game is worth picking up in a future sale.

text_cleaned: giving this a thumbs down because the story leans heavily on emotional manipulation, and the ending collapses under its own lack of logic. the game keeps insisting that pragmata is human, yet everything about the character contradicts that, making several key dialogues feel unintentionally silly. people often describe this as a dad simulator, but that framing doesn t hold up. the dynamic is reversed: hugh would barely survive the prologue without diana. she protects him, not the other way around. the narrative also reuses the same plot device capcom already employed for grace in re9 just months earlier, which makes the twist feel recycled rather than meaningful. the game is solid overall, but it doesn t bring anything new or original to the table, and its emotional beats feel engineered rather than earned. even the hacking mechanic is essentially a retread of what nier: automata already did years ago.

text_cleaned: ever since i saw the original announcement for pragmata back in 2020, i was intrigued by this mysterious game featuring an astronaut and a child android as protagonists. it was set to release in 2022, but an apology was issued stating that the game would be delayed to 2023. after what felt like an eternity of silence, a definitive release date was issued before being brought forward by a week . despite all of these delays, developmental setbacks and any other issues that may have impacted the project, pragmata has lived up to my hopes. after playing the sketchbook demo which you should play if you haven t already , i could see that capcom had managed to create an engaging, intuitive and dynamic combat system. the ability to control both a third person shooter and a hacking minigame simultaneously appears somewhat overwhelming at first, but this system is a prime example of how to increase depth and complexity to keep players engaged throughout the entire game. things start off simple at first with basic weapons and hacking abilities, but each zone of the game adds more layers to the engaging core. this keeps you on your toes, with new abilities and hazards building on top of the foundational mechanics for a constantly evolving challenge. the characters and their interactions is where pragmata truly shines. hugh and diana embody the lone wolf cub stereotype that has been very successful in stories such as the last of us and the modern god of war games. hugh presents a father figure to diana, being kind, reassuring and encouraging towards her. diana is easily distracted, rather goofy but steadfastly loyal to hugh. her personality is almost exactly like what you expect from a 6 year old discovering the world for the first time. capcom have done an incredible job at making both of the protagonists and their relationship charming and endearing. many times throughout the game i found myself smirking at their interactions. hugh and diana can be quite funny at times, but they never go too far with the jokes, sprinkling moments of brevity and light heartedness throughout. visually the game is fantastic, being yet another testament to the potential of the re engine, and it runs smoothly too. the game is well optimised and it shows. being able to boot up a brand new game, have it run well and be a fully finished, self contained experience is something of a novelty these days. this is a reminder of how games used to be. i would recommend pragmata to anyone who is interested in it. the game tells a great story with wonderful characters, and the gameplay is very innovative and engaging to boot. it s well worth your time to experience, and there s always the sketchbook demo for anyone who is unsure. h3 overall, pragmata is a heart warming and refreshingly positive experience, with a central focus on fatherhood. this is easily my goty for 2026. h3

text_cleaned: h1 it s okay capcom, we all make mistakes sometimes h1 pragmata turned out to be a game i never expected. the hacking shooting gameplay loop looked weird from the moment it was revealed, but i still thought it could be an interesting game. having played it, i can say it s one of the worst games of the post re engine era of capcom. the problems of this game aren t actually that many, but they are very deep and visceral. i just want to start by saying pragmata really does feel like a side project, and it does so in the worst way possible. over the course of the game, i really thought to myself, this game must ve been made from the scrapped parts of capcom s other projects. i don t know how true that is, but post re engine capcom games were already very resourceful in terms of reused assets, certain design philosophies, etc., but it feels like pragmata utilized a different approach. i was expecting the signature capcom stuff, but found leftovers or maybe scrapped afterthoughts from other games. this, to me, is the worst aspect of this game: it lacks identity, it s just a shadow of other games. it doesn t feel like its own thing, it s just a culmination of parts that were not worthy of other capcom games. now to be more specific, pragmata fails miserably in what capcom normally does best: moment to moment gameplay and level design. first of all, the level design is too simple and uninteresting for a game like this. if you want players to explore and do backtracking, you want your levels to feel coherent and fun to traverse. pragmata fails at what resident evil does best. again, i don t like to make direct comparisons, but when a game feels like a cheap version of another game from the same developer in certain aspects, i d like to mention that. the combat and moment to moment gameplay are too shallow and repetitive. the hacking shooting gameplay loop is an absolute mess. some people even compare it to a captcha for some reason, but i actually wish it were more akin to a captcha. hacking in this game is so mindless and overused. it doesn t add anything to the core gameplay, it s just there, for no reason whatsoever in terms of gameplay. hacking is at the game s core thematically, but in terms of game design, it is executed terribly. while the gunplay is serviceable, i can t really evaluate that aspect alone since it is very much interconnected with the other parts of core combat. weapon variety is definitely one of the positives since pretty much every weapon serves a purpose, but i d argue most weapons don t feel that satisfying to use. pragmata severely lacks any real challenge whatsoever. requiem was a mess in terms of difficulty already, but pragmata went a step further in terms of removing the difficulty and challenge. this type of game really needs better difficulty and challenge management. limited health items, ammo, weapons, etc. lose their meaning. i know the lunatic difficulty exists, but it is only available after the first playthrough and is meant to be an exotic challenge rather than a meaningful one that is supposed to be experienced on the first playthrough. the overall game design and structure are incredibly repetitive and, on top of it, very bland. platforming, looking for collectibles and resources, etc. are all way worse than i could ve imagined. it might sound like i am being extra harsh about this stuff just because the game s developer is capcom, but the problems go way deeper than expectations vs. reality. diana is very cute, but i never really cared for her if i m being honest. the lack of characterization for both diana and hugh is very disappointing. i wasn t expecting the next the last of us, but capcom, as always, managed to fumble the writing and gave us a very surface level story. considering the lack of over the top, exotic characters like dante, vergil, leon, chris, etc., it is perhaps the least interesting universe they have created. all in all, pragmata is a game that lacks identity and one that feels built on top of failed and previously discarded ideas. capcom has been on a roll recently, so i ll gladly give them a pass on this one. i have very high expectations for onimusha, and i hope they will end 2026 on a great note.

text_cleaned: like people have said many times before me i wish there was a middle of the road or meh option for reviews. to be honest i think this game is over hyped. its definitely not a bad game, but it doesn t feel worth the 60. especially considering it apparently is only 12 hours long granted i only played around an hour and made it past the first boss before i refunded it but i found the combat incredibly easy and not all that compelling. there is only two difficulty settings from the start which seem to be easy and somehow even easier . apparently there is a third harder difficulty but it is locked behind beating the game for some reason? the enemies that i encountered move at the pace of a snail and all seem to have a single attack, so the gameplay loop boils down to slowly back away from an enemy while you hack them, and then shoot the weak point that opens until they die. i understand that combat doesn t really seem to be the primary focus here, its more about the story and the characters, however i also found that to be fairly generic. the game didn t really give me a reason to care about any of these people. a majority of what was gonna happen i immediately guessed within minutes of starting the game. maybe this game just isn t for me and that s fine. i figured i might as well throw my two cents in for anyone like me who might be disappointed with how incredibly easy and somewhat generic i found this to be. the optimization is good and it runs well on linux though so props for that.

text_cleaned: to start off, amazing story and very likeable characters. there is no doubt about that. but moving on, there is very little else in this game. the game part of this game is not bad but is horridly mediocre. the game plays like something i would have expected back in the xbox 360 or gamecube days. movement is choppy, you will frequently want to clamber and the character just sort of won t or they do and it is a painfully slow animation every single time. there is no sense of momentum so moving platforms will be jarring to jump from as they will slip out from under you the moment you press the button. the worst part is the combat. if this game had simply used a proper ammo system and made hacking a bonus ability it would be fine, but that did not happen. instead, what we get is a forced iphone tier minigame blocking up the right side of your screen every single enemy. it rapidly grows tedious and outright annoying in rooms dense with targets as it can be easy to lose track of exactly which enemy your screen displays a hack currently on. this is made drastically worse by most enemies just being walking damage sponges, even when exposed by a hack. sadly, this game has an utterly bizarre take on ammo economy. your primary weapon recharges but all others are very low ammo capacity use and discard style weapons. this will frequently create a problem where enemies are not dangerous enough to warrant using the very few shots in your shotgun or rocket launcher but your primary will not be able to reliably kill enemies in one charge. this results in you frequently just staring at enemies as they zombie walk at you, twiddling your thumbs and checking your watch, as you wait for the gun to recharge. this makes what could have been more like dead space or resident evil 4 into a boring waiting game in most fights, as once you only have a single enemy or two left they pose no real threat beyond a direct assault on your patience while they just tank round and after round that you slowly watch recharge. as for music, i would not say it is bad but they went for a mostly subdued style so i can recall very little of the actual tracks. being that the story and characters are the best part of this game i would recommend just watching a youtube playthrough rather than suffering from such mediocre gameplay.

text_cleaned: i was initially hesitant on this game, i ended up reading reviews on steam and changed my mind and i m glad i did. the characters hugh and diana have entertaining interactions and it fun to see them grow closer. hugh was a refreshing main character, he isn t some some jaded grizzled war hero, spouting marvel one liners. he is wholesome, kind and talks about his family which i appreciated. diana is an endearing character who avoids all the pit falls normally associated with kid characters. she is more child like then robot which i expected but it was executed well. their story was interesting and i was always excited to see what came next. what immediately hooked me was the combat system, it is such a refreshing take on the 3rd person shooter. hugh is controlled from an over the shoulder point of view and has multiple different weapons which all feel unique. the bad guys you encounter needed to be hacked to open their weak point and that is where diana comes in. there is grid system that you use the face buttons of your controller to move to the goal to hack the robot. it is such a fun and engaging combat system and not something i have seen before. diana can get mods that change what some of her hacking does and other things that kept this gameplay loop fresh. i used a wireless bluetooth ps5 controller, the haptic triggers and gyro aiming really added to my overall enjoyment. the music, environment, gameplay, story, voice acting was all top tier. i was able to play at the highest settings and thought the game looked amazing. i played this right after re9 and capcom is on a heck of a run right now. right now this is my top contender for game of the year, i highly recommend this game.

text_cleaned: i came away from pragmata more mixed than i expected. early on, i was pretty positive, mostly because the demo sold me on the combat. shooting and hacking felt slick, immediate, and unusually natural on mouse and keyboard. but the more i played, the more i realized the gameplay friction is not really an input issue but a design issue. if the game wants you to hack in real time while also rewarding you for keeping pressure on the same enemy, it creates a split focus combat loop that can feel more awkward than smart. the real collapse, though, is the writing. the dialogue is terrible, the characters feel preprogrammed, and there s barely any mystery to the story apart from wtf happened to the people and idus. the villain and the central objective show up almost immediately, so there is very little intrigue. what really kills it for me is how hugh and diana behave from the very start. hugh does not react like a human being who just got thrown into a bizarre and dangerous situation involving hostile ai. he almost instantly slides into protective father mode. diana, meanwhile, immediately acts like a ready made little girl archetype, all innocence, wonder, and manufactured childlike behavior. so instead of a tense, suspicious, unstable dynamic, the game rushes straight into something sentimental and artificial. it feels fake within minutes. that is why my verdict has cooled hard. i still think pragmata is fun to play. the mechanics do enough heavy lifting to keep me going. but narratively it feels hollow. there is no real hook, no reveal worth chewing on, and no sense that the story is building toward anything memorable. i can enjoy playing it, sure. i just do not expect to remember it for anything beyond the gameplay. if this game didn t already have a 97 overwhelmingly positive rating, i would ve recommended this game for the gameplay alone. but i do not think this game deserves that 97 praise for reasons stated above, so my thumbs down is a way to counteract this falsehood.

lighting is great, and the atmosphere is strong. it s not loud or over the top sci fi it s quiet, a bit lonely, and slightly creepy. you re just walking on the moon like you accidentally ended up there and now you have to deal with it you play as hugh, who lowkey looks like samuel rodrigues from metal gear rising revengeance same serious face, same energy. then there s diana, your small android companion who is honestly the best part of the game. she s cute, useful, and not annoying, which is already a huge win at some point you ll notice hugh s neo bushido suit if you ve bought the deluxe edition and think wait a minute. this thing looks like it came straight out of kojima productions same kind of futuristic robot vibe, like it could walk into another game and nobody would question it. not complaining though, it looks cool the relationship between hugh and diana is what keeps things interesting. it s not forced or overly emotional. just small moments here and there, and somehow you start caring without even realizing it gameplay is where it gets a bit spicy. it s not just shooting everything that moves. you have to mix hacking with combat, and it actually flows better than expected enemy design is pretty solid too. not just basic enemies running at you. some actually make you think for a second instead the boss fights omg there s also way more side content than expected i thought it would be a straight story. some side stuff is genuinely interesting, some of it is just there to keep you busy, but overall it adds more depth the world building is handled nicely as well. it doesn t dump everything on you with long boring explanations you kind of piece things together as you go, which makes it more engaging sound design deserves a mention too the silence, the small background noises, everything adds to that lonely moon feeling pacing is probably the one area where it slips a bit some parts feel perfect and really pull you in, and then some sections just go on a little too long it doesn t ruin the experience, but you ll definitely notice it overall, it feels like a game that actually tried to do something different instead of playing it safe it s not perfect, but it has its own vibe, and that makes it stand out and yeah, the ending completely unexpected lowkey yeah. enough to make a grown man cry

text_cleaned: pragmata easily lands itself as one of the best new ips i ve played in a very long time. the gameplay is very solid for a new system, with the hacking mixed in with combat being quite the challenge at times. that mix and multitasking of traditional shooting and mid battle hacking really encourages you to use all the tools at your disposal, especially when you get caught up in areas where multiple enemies will come at you all at once. and since these tools and weapons don t operate with your traditional reloading and ammo pickups, but rather a replace your weapon once you run out kind of system, you re put in a position where you have to constantly adapt as your build changes over the course of each journey until you return to the shelter. story wise, the game seamlessly incorporates multiple layers of the plot into one cohesive narrative. diana s origin as a pragmata unit, her relation to the mysterious eight, all the malfunctions and dead filament, delphi and their own shady actions which led to the events of the story. all of these were masterfully crafted and fleshed out to tie the whole story of pragmata together. and most importantly, the relationship between hugh and diana, being the anchor that carries the great emotional weight of the current events, is what really hooks you into the entire experience and keeps you engaged with the story at all times. players didn t call it a dad simulator for nothing. once again, pragmata is very easily one of the best new ips of the year, maybe even in the past decade of aaa games. if you re into games with great stories that can walk the talk with engaging and challenging gameplay, then i would highly recommend pragmata. review ends here my only gripe minor spoilers ahead : spoiler the extension to the ending is... quite disappointing relative to the amount of things the player needs to do in order to get the empty black screen with a very short and quite vague line of dialogue that only hints at a variation from the first ending. i sure hope this is a way of baiting an upcoming expansion to the story because that felt like it was all for nothing for me. and i definitely would not mind an expansion. spoiler

text_cleaned: a great game. and i really want to highlight to the game part, as far too many videogames these days want to sell themselves broadly as experiences, instead of leaning into the medium that they occupy. first, this is a moderately budgeted single player third person shooter using a brand new ip. 15 years ago, this would have been a passe observation, but now that s a fairly unique concept right off the go. the main selling point is that instead of your guns being your sole and primary method of death dealing, you are required to hack enemies to expose weakpoints. the method of doing so is very well integrated; you use the face buttons to navigate the hack screen and the left stick to move around and create spacing. spacing is key, as you ll often find yourselves in close quarters, so you are encouraged to stay mobile and prioritize enemies when things get hairy. the big element i d like to highlight is the progression system. it s non indicative, so you actually don t know how much room is left to grow from the jump, almost as if the game wants you to explore and discover new stuff along with the main character. additionally, the game goes out of its way to tempt you with collectables, challenges, and multiple in game currencies to purchase upgrades. it s a rewarding system. the story is also enjoyable and well written, though doesn t have any real twists. if you have any expectations that the plot will be subverted, you will be disappointed. i stuck it out though, and was happy overall. visual and audio are good. i like the direction of the game, and while i don t think either are unforgettable, i can t complain and won t say they were ever bad, inappropriate for the mood, or distracting. performance was fine at 1440p. i wasn t paying attention to framerate, but i did get some hitches here and there. overall, 8.5 10, maybe a 9 10 depending on my mood. definitely pick this one up if you re interested. i really hope that the strong performance of this ip encourages the creation of more properties that aren t based on already existing series .

text_cleaned: a refreshingly fun game! is it a good game? definitely yes! ... is it really as good as all the hype says? no. interesting game mechanic where you have to balance accurate shooting and puzzle solving simultaneously. i like the challenge of choosing which abilities to avoid or use while hacking on the fly. controller is the only way to play the game, which can bring an extra challenge for people like me who are used to mouse and keyboard. however, considering the long development time i expected maybe a bit more depth from the story, and a bit more lenght from the game. then again doing the same thing over an over would probably get boring, hence i like the introduction of a new late game mechanic that makes the combat a bit more interesting again. i would have liked to see more of that, more mechanics to build more depth. performance wise the game is solid, but my computer is high end so it is hard to judge how it runs on more average pc s. underline: the game is technically very solid and extremely fun to play. if you like games that are actually games and not just brainless time consumption programs, this is an easy buy!

text_cleaned: well, this one sorta took me by surprise. seeing the trailer for it at the game awards didn t leave any real impression on me at the time and the combat system displayed left me skeptical on how it would actually work in action. but, capcom ended up nailing it and creating one of the most addictive and unique combat systems i ve ever played intertwined within a story that is heartwarming, sweet and human affirming; something i appreciate more and more these days. it s a very linear game with some backtracking as you unlock abilities throughout. you won t be straying far from the path, but you will have to be aware of your surroundings as there are items and upgrades all around that take some extra effort to get to. the combat is where the game really shines for me. there s a good amount of variety here for experimentation and playstyle tailoring with a training and ng system to keep you busy after you complete your first playthrough. it is slower and more tactical, but that is a good thing to me, and is incredibly satisfying one you get the hang of it. it s just so much fun, and the synergy between hugh and diana is so well done. great weapon variety as well. i focused on hacking, movement and close quarters combat primarily. sound design very good. the music is pretty subtle but fits well. it has an old school, n64 ps1 vibe to it and reminds me of older games and anime. i loved the jazz track and couldn t help think of a spike spiegel fight scene on initially hearing it. i dig it alot. performance i have everything jacked to the gills with dlss quality, ray tracing on, path off, and frame gen off. it s very smooth on my pc and looks incredible. negatives for me are: 1. difficulty. i know it s just normal, but even the last boss was just too easy. i wish there was a higher difficulty selection up front. 2. hugh s story kind of takes a backsteat to diana s, and i completely forgot about it at one point. this made certain areas feel rushed to me and hugh insignificant at times. 3. it has some of that japanese cheeseball dialog, which kind of detracts from some scenes, but i do find it charming since i grew up playing rpgs. 4. small gripe, but the soundtrack you select at the shelter should stick and replay every time you revisit instead of reverting to the default. i m glad to hear capcom is going to continue pragmata as an ip, because it absolutely deserves it. my fav of 2026 and is probably going to creep into my top 10 of all time because of how much i love the combat.

text_cleaned: with this years release of re9 and pragmata, capcom has made it pretty obvious that they have found the secret to providing what people desire the most. i ve only heard about pragmata fairly recently, shortly after the release, and with the whole internet completely out of their minds over diana, it made me curious. as someone who has never really enjoyed space oriented games, this game completely blew me away. pragmata features probably the most unique combat mechanic i ve ever experienced, hugh being the main force when it comes to shooting, with diana doing the hacking, they make a perfect duo. the hacking part might seem little bit overwhelming when you start the game for the first game, but you can certainly get used to it pretty fast, with the game providing plenty of things to make it easier for you. i was happy with how diana was made, even though she s not a human, she still does all the kid stuff, like being silly and asking lots of questions pretty frequently. which makes the game that much more heartwarming. the addition of having the option to play hide n seek with her whenever you feel like it, makes it that much nicer. without a doubt, the game s design is just stunning. enemy environmental designs are incredible. there has been plenty of moments, where i just stopped for a while and observed how amazing the game looks. the game runs pretty smoothly and i ve not experienced any issues whatsoever. you have the freedom to create many different builds based on what feels the most effective to you, with the game constantly providing you with new weapons, mods and hacking nodes. finally, i d like to happily say that even though capcom is certainly not known for their narrative execution in games, i truly believe they ve done a solid job in pragmata. i can t tell you that it s the best story you ll ever experience, but it certainly was amazing. i loved every second of the full playthrough and i m sure it won t end there for me.

text_cleaned: after so many years of development hell, pragmata is genuinely one of the most amazing starts for a brand new capcom ip in such a long time. this game is some grade a cinema from the amazing gameplay to the absolute wholesome and heartwarming narrative, to the point where i basically 100 everything i could with it just to get more. the gameplay seems obtuse at first but once you really understand it the combat is super addicting to play around with. moving around and shooting hacking felt perfect which can be very difficult when you got a heavy player character and trying to balance feeling weighty but also fast enough where it doesn t become a hinderance. hacking enemies felt like one of the best ways to implementing a sort of minigame mid fight i ve ever seen, requiring you to solve small puzzles before inflicting one. bouncing from this to regular shooting felt so seamless that i easily locked into an amazing flow state fighting a tons of enemies. i agree on the story being simple with not a lot of groundbreaking twists and turns but sometimes that s just what you need. capcom went for a found family narrative and made sure as hell to hammer in on that emotional journey. every moment from the high octane set pieces to the grounded small yet cute scenes between the two leads had me scavenging to see everything i can. the hugh and diana family dynamic seriously carried the whole game. easily one of my gotys of 2026 and we re not even half way through it yet. the capcom 2026 generational run is moving at full force and i m all here for it.

text_cleaned: h3 pragmata h3 is an interesting third person shooter in a sci fi setting. guns, robots, hacking, bossfights, action, all things made challenging but also well balanced, and all of that has a story that carries you forward. hugh, the protagonist of the story, loses his entire team to find himself in a hostile environment, but gets help from an unexpected individual, an android with the appearance of a little girl and the power to hack through the defensive systems of the enemies. the games strength is definitely the environments and the fights inside of them, where the beginning has just a small variety of robots, it later adds more and more to the mix, to keep the entire game very challenging to the player. but the game also offers a lot of different weapons, and modules in return and allows the player to upgrade them too, which opens a lot of ways on how to deal with the threat of the evil robot army. also its nice to see, that the developers used the re engine which, at least on my pc, was running very smooth on high settings no upscaler, no fg and still above 100 fps. but i have to be fair and also mention a negative point of games to, and in the case of pragmata it s the low amount of boss encounters, by the way they introduced the robots they could have gone much more into different boss designs to throw at the player. also the slow reload of the main weapon is often than just a little bit, annoying in some fights. and the default button layout for keyboard and mouse is not the best, as with a later unlock of an ability, the game wants the player to keep e pressed to charge an attack, while at the same time requires the player to use w,a,s,d to still keep dodging incoming attacks. as humans we don t have that many fingers on one hand :p . but all in all, this game is a very good third person sci fi robot shooter and i can recommend this game to everyone who like action packed fights and third person shooters. h3 points i give this game average points 8,25 10 : h3 8,4 10 graphic good for a game and runs well even on high settings, but still room for improvements 7 10 art style the robot designs are nice, but also kinda basic and repetitive after a while, they could have gone a bit into more here, also the environments feel often a bit to clean, sure it s a moon base, but design wise, a lot of levels feels like training areas rather than a base where humans once lived and worked 7,5 10 atmosphere the game tries to tell that there once where humans, but it does not really make much use of it, like trying to give the player the feeling of humans died here! , which it should have to fit more to the overall story, but the robots feel threatening. 8,6 10 gameplay robots, weapons, action, at the most part perfect, but still a little bit changes could have been done to make the weapon switch during combat a bit more fluid 7,8 10 main story solid story that drives the player forward and tells what has actually happened on that moon base 9,2 10 characters the characters in the game are well written for their part and fit well in their role and the setting. what they could have done tho, would be to have a few more characters there, like one of the crew survived and shows up later or something like that. 7 10 soundtrack not the worst soundtracks, they fit to the combat situation, but are not so the over the top once like a rare amount of games was able to accomplish 10 10 performance was running very well on my pc, no crashes, no stuttering, had everything on highest settings, and had turned off upscaler and fg and still constantly above 100 fps 8,8 10 content besides the main story and maps, there is also a lot of side content to discover, like training missions, collectables, conversations with the other character in the main hub, i hope they add in more content in the future to expand on the moon base and possible adventures there

text_cleaned: 10 10 pragmata delivers one of the most innovative third person shooter experiences i have played in years. the dual character system is the standout. controlling hugh for movement and gunplay while using diana for real time hacking creates a constant decision loop. you cannot simply shoot your way through encounters. you have to expose weak points first, which forces you to think under pressure and makes every fight feel deliberate. the gunplay itself is excellent. it has that fast, responsive, skill based feel similar to vanquish, where positioning, timing and movement matter just as much as accuracy. you are always juggling shooting, dodging and hacking, and once it clicks the combat flows very well. it never felt clunky or restrictive. enemy design supports this system well. robots are not just damage sponges. each one pushes you to engage with the hacking mechanic properly, otherwise you are punished for trying to brute force encounters. boss fights especially benefit from this and feel much fairer once you have upgraded your gear and understood the mechanics. the progression system ties into this nicely. i spent around 20 hours completing the main story while going for 100 collectables and replaying sectors to farm luminum for upgrades. this made a noticeable difference in later encounters and boss fights. you can finish the game in roughly 8 hours if you rush it, but it would be a waste given how strong the gameplay loop is. there is also post game content and higher difficulties which i still need to finish. visually, it is one of the strongest uses of the re engine so far. character detail stands out, especially with hair rendering, and the moon setting has a clean, atmospheric look that fits the tone well. for a new ip developed by a newer capcom team, it feels very polished and worth the wait since its original ps5 announcement. the story is fairly standard with a father and daughter dynamic, but it does its job. there are some good twists tied to the cradle incident on the moon that keep it engaging without overcomplicating things. the gameplay can feel repetitive if you do not engage with the depth of the system, but it is designed around mastery. the more you improve, the better it feels. i had no issues with keyboard and mouse despite some complaints and found it easier than using a controller. once you have fully completed everything there is not much reason to return, but that is typical for longer single player games. fantastic crisis scenario set on the moon with astronaut suits, robots and a small android. easily one of the standout releases for me.

text_cleaned: it s been worth every minute of the wait! it s not over the 60, for one thing. it s got a slow but compelling combat and exploration experience. like dead space but... not dreary and depressing heh. in fact, depending on your tolerance for sugary sweet characters, diana is an adorable little sprig. as a life long sci fi buff my concept of person is not limited to species, or even biology, and as a video game character diana fits well into the trope of a machine shaped as a person becoming a person. in terms of gameplay, again, it s a methodical, mild paced sci fi adventure with a unique and compelling combat gimmick. in dead space it was needing to remove parts of your enemy, in pragmata, you use the face buttons to play a brief hybrid of snake and minesweeper to make the enemy more prone to damage. it s a bit tricky but once it lands it feels solid and the boost button adds a bit of leeway that dead space by design does not possess. i know a lot of people out there are going to compare this to dead space, and it s partly fair. it s just the thing for people like me who want to play in a weighty suit with powerful tools and not be depressed doing it : oh and side note, don t be scared of the win11 requirement, i have a mid scale win10 spec and i run the game okay
